🟠 Ubuntu / Debian (APT)
Bash Ubuntu/Debian Installation 
# Step 1: Install dependencies
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y gnupg software-properties-common

# Step 2: Add HashiCorp GPG key
wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | \
    gpg --dearmor | \
    sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg > /dev/null

# Step 3: Verify key fingerprint
gpg --no-default-keyring \
    --keyring /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg \
    --fingerprint

# Step 4: Add HashiCorp repository
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] \
    https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" | \
    sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list

# Step 5: Install Terraform
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install terraform

# Step 6: Verify
terraform --version
🔴 CentOS / RHEL / Fedora (DNF/YUM)
Bash RHEL/CentOS/Fedora Installation 
# Step 1: Install yum-config-manager
sudo yum install -y yum-utils

# Step 2: Add HashiCorp repository
sudo yum-config-manager --add-repo https://rpm.releases.hashicorp.com/RHEL/hashicorp.repo

# Step 3: Install Terraform
sudo yum -y install terraform

# For Fedora using DNF:
sudo dnf install -y dnf-plugins-core
sudo dnf config-manager --add-repo https://rpm.releases.hashicorp.com/fedora/hashicorp.repo
sudo dnf -y install terraform

# Verify
terraform --version
🟡 Amazon Linux 2
Bash Amazon Linux 2 Installation 
# Add HashiCorp repository
sudo yum install -y yum-utils
sudo yum-config-manager --add-repo https://rpm.releases.hashicorp.com/AmazonLinux/hashicorp.repo

# Install Terraform
sudo yum -y install terraform

# Verify
terraform version
📦 Universal Binary (Any Linux)

Works on any Linux distribution:

Bash Universal Linux Installation 
# Set version
TERRAFORM_VERSION="1.9.5"

# Detect architecture
ARCH=$(uname -m)
case $ARCH in
    x86_64) ARCH="amd64" ;;
    aarch64) ARCH="arm64" ;;
    armv7l) ARCH="arm" ;;
esac

# Download
wget "https://releases.hashicorp.com/terraform/${TERRAFORM_VERSION}/terraform_${TERRAFORM_VERSION}_linux_${ARCH}.zip"

# Install unzip if needed
sudo apt-get install unzip -y || sudo yum install unzip -y

# Extract
unzip terraform_${TERRAFORM_VERSION}_linux_${ARCH}.zip

# Move to PATH
sudo mv terraform /usr/local/bin/

# Set permissions
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/terraform

# Verify
terraform --version

# Cleanup
rm terraform_${TERRAFORM_VERSION}_linux_${ARCH}.zip
🐳

2.1.4 Docker Installation

🐳 Containerized Terraform

Running Terraform in Docker is ideal for CI/CD pipelines, ensuring consistent environments, and avoiding local installation.

📦 Official HashiCorp Image
Bash Docker Commands 
# Pull the official Terraform image
docker pull hashicorp/terraform:latest

# Pull specific version
docker pull hashicorp/terraform:1.9.5

# Run Terraform commands
docker run --rm hashicorp/terraform:1.9.5 --version

# Run with current directory mounted
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/workspace -w /workspace hashicorp/terraform:1.9.5 init

# Run plan with AWS credentials
docker run --rm \
    -v $(pwd):/workspace \
    -w /workspace \
    -e AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID \
    -e AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY \
    -e AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1 \
    hashicorp/terraform:1.9.5 plan

# Run apply (non-interactive)
docker run --rm \
    -v $(pwd):/workspace \
    -w /workspace \
    -e AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID \
    -e AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY \
    hashicorp/terraform:1.9.5 apply -auto-approve
⚡ Create Shell Alias

Create an alias to use Docker Terraform like the native CLI:

Bash ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc 
# Add to your shell profile
alias terraform='docker run --rm -it \
    -v $(pwd):/workspace \
    -w /workspace \
    -v $HOME/.aws:/root/.aws:ro \
    -e AWS_PROFILE=${AWS_PROFILE:-default} \
    hashicorp/terraform:1.9.5'

# Reload shell
source ~/.bashrc

# Now use terraform normally
terraform --version
terraform init
terraform plan
📋 Docker Compose Setup

For project-based Terraform with Docker Compose:

YAML docker-compose.yml 
version: '3.8'

services:
  terraform:
    image: hashicorp/terraform:1.9.5
    working_dir: /workspace
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - ~/.aws:/root/.aws:ro
    environment:
      - AWS_PROFILE=${AWS_PROFILE:-default}
      - TF_LOG=${TF_LOG:-}
    entrypoint: ["terraform"]

# Usage:
# docker-compose run --rm terraform init
# docker-compose run --rm terraform plan
# docker-compose run --rm terraform apply
⚠️
SECURITY: Never embed AWS credentials in Docker images. Use environment variables, IAM roles, or mount credentials as read-only volumes.
✅

2.1.5 Version Verification

✅ Verify Your Installation

After installation, verify Terraform is working correctly and check the installed version.

🔢 Version Commands
Bash Checking Terraform Version 
# Short version
terraform --version

# Same as above
terraform -v

# Detailed version info (in a Terraform project directory)
terraform version

# JSON output for scripting
terraform version -json
terraform --version
Terraform v1.9.5
on linux_amd64
terraform version -json
{
  "terraform_version": "1.9.5",
  "platform": "linux_amd64",
  "provider_selections": {},
  "terraform_outdated": false
}
📋 Installation Verification Checklist
Bash Verification Script 
#!/bin/bash
# terraform-verify.sh - Verify Terraform installation

echo "=== Terraform Installation Verification ==="
echo ""

# Check if terraform is in PATH
if command -v terraform &> /dev/null; then
    echo "✅ Terraform is installed"
    echo "   Location: $(which terraform)"
else
    echo "❌ Terraform not found in PATH"
    exit 1
fi

# Get version
VERSION=$(terraform --version | head -n1)
echo "✅ $VERSION"

# Check minimum version (1.0+)
MIN_VERSION="1.0.0"
INSTALLED_VERSION=$(terraform --version -json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.terraform_version' 2>/dev/null || terraform --version | head -n1 | cut -d'v' -f2 | cut -d' ' -f1)

echo "✅ Installed version: $INSTALLED_VERSION"

# Test terraform help
if terraform -help > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "✅ Terraform help command works"
else
    echo "❌ Terraform help command failed"
    exit 1
fi

# Check platform
PLATFORM=$(terraform --version | grep "on " | awk '{print $2}')
echo "✅ Platform: $PLATFORM"

echo ""
echo "=== All checks passed! Terraform is ready to use. ==="
Expected Output
=== Terraform Installation Verification ===

✅ Terraform is installed
   Location: /usr/local/bin/terraform
✅ Terraform v1.9.5
✅ Installed version: 1.9.5
✅ Terraform help command works
✅ Platform: linux_amd64

=== All checks passed! Terraform is ready to use. ===
🧪 Quick Functionality Test

Create a minimal test to verify Terraform works:

Bash Create Test Configuration 
# Create test directory
mkdir terraform-test && cd terraform-test

# Create minimal configuration
cat > main.tf <<'EOF'
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.0.0"
}

output "hello" {
  value = "Terraform is working!"
}
EOF

# Initialize (downloads no providers for this simple test)
terraform init

# Apply the configuration
terraform apply -auto-approve

# You should see: hello = "Terraform is working!"

# Clean up
cd .. && rm -rf terraform-test
Expected Output
Initializing the backend...

Initializing provider plugins...

Terraform has been successfully initialized!

Changes to Outputs:
  + hello = "Terraform is working!"

Apply complete! Resources: 0 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed.

Outputs:

hello = "Terraform is working!"
📝
EXAM TIP: The exam may ask about terraform version vs terraform --version. Both work, but terraform version shows more details when run inside a project directory (including provider versions).

2.2 tfenv - Terraform Version Manager

HANDS-ON BEST PRACTICE
🎯

What You're Doing Here

Real-world teams often work on multiple projects that use different Terraform versions. Instead of manually reinstalling Terraform each time, you'll learn to use tfenv - a version manager that lets you install and switch between versions instantly. This is a professional best practice that will save you countless hours.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Have tfenv installed and working
  • Know how to install multiple Terraform versions
  • Use .terraform-version files for automatic version selection
🔄

🎛️ Version Management Made Easy

tfenv allows you to manage multiple Terraform versions, switch between them seamlessly, and ensure each project uses the correct version automatically.

  • Install multiple Terraform versions
  • Switch versions per-project or globally
  • Automatic version selection via .terraform-version
  • Essential for teams with multiple projects
10+ Versions Managed
1 Command to Switch
.terraform-version Auto-Selection File
INSTALL
⬇️

tfenv install

Install Terraform versions. Use "latest" or specific version like "1.9.5".

SWITCH
🔄

tfenv use

Switch active version. Instantly switch between installed versions.

LIST
📋

tfenv list

Show installed versions. Asterisk marks the currently active version.

REMOTE
🌐

tfenv list-remote

Show available versions. Lists all versions available for download.

AUTO
🎯

.terraform-version

Auto-select version per project. Just create file with version number.

DELETE
🗑️

tfenv uninstall

Remove old versions. Free up disk space from unused versions.

⬇️

2.2.1 Installing tfenv

📦 Installation Methods

tfenv is available for macOS and Linux. For Windows, use tfenv through WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux).

🍎 macOS (Homebrew)
Bash Install tfenv on macOS 
# Install tfenv via Homebrew
brew install tfenv

# Verify installation
tfenv --version

# Note: If you previously installed Terraform via Homebrew,
# uninstall it first to avoid conflicts:
# brew uninstall terraform
🐧 Linux / WSL
Bash Install tfenv on Linux 
# Clone tfenv repository
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/tfutils/tfenv.git ~/.tfenv

# Add to PATH (add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc)
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# Or for Zsh
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc

# Verify
tfenv --version
Expected Output
tfenv 3.0.0
🎮

2.2.2 Using tfenv

🎛️ Essential Commands

Learn to list, install, and switch between Terraform versions.

📋 tfenv Commands Reference
Bash Listing Versions 
# List all available remote versions
tfenv list-remote

# List all installed versions
tfenv list

# Show currently active version
tfenv version-name
Bash Installing Versions 
# Install latest version
tfenv install latest

# Install specific version
tfenv install 1.9.5

# Install latest 1.8.x version
tfenv install latest:^1.8

# Install version matching constraint
tfenv install min-required  # Based on required_version in .tf files
Bash Switching Versions 
# Use specific version globally
tfenv use 1.9.5

# Use latest installed version
tfenv use latest

# Use minimum required version from .tf files
tfenv use min-required

# Verify the switch
terraform version
tfenv list
  1.9.5
  1.8.5
  1.7.5
* 1.6.6 (set by /home/user/.tfenv/version)
  1.5.7
🗑️ Uninstalling Versions
Bash Remove Terraform Versions 
# Uninstall specific version
tfenv uninstall 1.5.7

# List installed to see what can be removed
tfenv list
📄

2.2.3 .terraform-version File

📌 Project Version Pinning

Create a .terraform-version file in your project root to automatically use the correct version.

📄 Automatic Version Selection

When you run any terraform command, tfenv checks for .terraform-version in the current directory and parent directories.

Bash Creating .terraform-version 
# Create .terraform-version file
echo "1.9.5" > .terraform-version

# Or for latest
echo "latest" > .terraform-version

# Or with version constraint
echo "latest:^1.8" > .terraform-version

# Now terraform will use this version automatically
terraform version
Text .terraform-version Examples 
# Exact version
1.9.5

# Latest version
latest

# Latest within major version
latest:^1

# Latest within minor version
latest:^1.9

# Minimum required (from .tf files)
min-required
💡
BEST PRACTICE: Always commit .terraform-version to your repository. This ensures all team members and CI/CD pipelines use the same Terraform version.
⭐

2.2.4 tfenv Best Practices

📌
Pin Versions

Always use .terraform-version to pin versions per project

🔒
Exact Versions

Use exact versions in production, not latest

👥
Team Sync

Ensure all team members install tfenv

🔄
Regular Updates

Periodically update to newer versions after testing

📁 Example Project Setup
Bash Setting Up a New Project 
# Create new project
mkdir my-terraform-project && cd my-terraform-project

# Set Terraform version for this project
echo "1.9.5" > .terraform-version

# Install if not already installed
tfenv install

# Verify
terraform version

# Create initial configuration
cat > versions.tf <<'EOF'
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.9.0, < 2.0.0"
  
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}
EOF

# Initialize
terraform init

2.3 IDE Setup and Productivity

HANDS-ON PRODUCTIVITY
🎯

What You're Doing Here

Writing Terraform code is much easier with a properly configured IDE. In this section, you'll set up VS Code with essential extensions that provide syntax highlighting, auto-completion, error detection, and automatic formatting. These tools will make you significantly more productive and help you catch errors before running Terraform.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Have VS Code configured with Terraform extensions
  • Get real-time code validation as you type
  • Have auto-formatting on save
  • Know keyboard shortcuts to speed up development
💻

🛠️ Supercharge Your Development

A properly configured IDE dramatically improves productivity with syntax highlighting, auto-completion, validation, and formatting.

  • VS Code - Most popular choice with rich extensions
  • JetBrains IDEs - Full-featured HCL support
  • Vim/Neovim - Lightweight with plugins
  • LSP support for any editor
VS Code Recommended IDE
6 Key Extensions
LSP Protocol Support
2-space Indent Standard
POPULAR
🔵

VS Code

Free, lightweight, rich extensions. Official HashiCorp extension with full LSP support.

PRO
🧠

JetBrains IDEs

IntelliJ, PyCharm, WebStorm. Deep code analysis with Terraform plugin.

FAST
📟

Vim / Neovim

Terminal-based, keyboard-driven. vim-terraform plugin + LSP config.

MINIMAL
📝

Sublime Text

Lightweight editor with Terraform syntax plug-in. Simple and fast.

🔵

2.3.1 VS Code Setup

🔵 Visual Studio Code

VS Code is the most popular editor for Terraform development due to its rich extension ecosystem and excellent HCL support.

📦 Essential Extensions
Bash Install Extensions via CLI 
# HashiCorp Terraform Extension (Official)
code --install-extension hashicorp.terraform

# HashiCorp HCL Extension
code --install-extension hashicorp.hcl

# Terraform doc snippets
code --install-extension run-at-scale.terraform-doc-snippets

# GitLens (for version control)
code --install-extension eamodio.gitlens

# YAML support (for CI/CD configs)
code --install-extension redhat.vscode-yaml

# Rainbow CSV (for tfvars)
code --install-extension mechatroner.rainbow-csv
🏷️ hashicorp.terraform

Syntax highlighting, intellisense, formatting, validation, code navigation

📝 terraform-doc-snippets

Quick snippets for common Terraform patterns and AWS resources

🔍 GitLens

Git blame, history, comparisons inline in your editor

⚙️ Recommended Settings
JSON settings.json 
{
  // Terraform-specific settings
  "[terraform]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.formatOnSaveMode": "file"
  },
  "[terraform-vars]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true
  },
  
  // Terraform Language Server
  "terraform.languageServer.enable": true,
  "terraform.validation.enableEnhancedValidation": true,
  "terraform.codelens.referenceCount": true,
  
  // File associations
  "files.associations": {
    "*.tf": "terraform",
    "*.tfvars": "terraform-vars",
    "*.tfstate": "json"
  },
  
  // Editor settings for HCL
  "editor.tabSize": 2,
  "editor.insertSpaces": true,
  "editor.wordWrap": "off",
  
  // Auto-save
  "files.autoSave": "afterDelay",
  "files.autoSaveDelay": 1000
}
🔮

2.3.2 HashiCorp Extension Features

🎨
Syntax Highlighting

Full HCL syntax highlighting with theme support

💡
IntelliSense

Auto-completion for resources, attributes, and functions

🚨
Validation

Real-time error detection before running terraform

📐
Formatting

Auto-format on save using terraform fmt

🧭
Navigation

Go to definition, find references, symbols outline

📖
Hover Docs

Documentation on hover for providers and resources

🔮 IntelliSense in Action
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  main.tf                                        [Terraform] │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  resource "aws_" ▌                                          │
│            ┌────────────────────────────────┐               │
│            │ aws_instance                   │               │
│            │ aws_s3_bucket                  │               │
│            │ aws_vpc                        │               │
│            │ aws_subnet                     │               │
│            │ aws_security_group             │               │
│            │ aws_iam_role                   │               │
│            │ aws_lambda_function            │               │
│            │ ...150+ more resources         │               │
│            └────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                             │
│  VS Code showing AWS provider resource suggestions          │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🧠

2.3.3 JetBrains IDEs

🧠 JetBrains Support

IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, and other JetBrains IDEs support Terraform through the Terraform/HCL plugin.

📦 Plugin Installation
Step 1: Open Settings

Navigate to File → Settings → Plugins (Windows/Linux) or Preferences → Plugins (macOS)

Step 2: Search Marketplace

Search for "Terraform" or "HCL" in the Marketplace tab

Step 3: Install Plugin

Install "Terraform and HCL" by JetBrains

Step 4: Restart IDE

Restart the IDE to activate the plugin

📟

2.3.4 Vim/Neovim Setup

📟 vim-terraform Plugin
Vim ~/.vimrc or init.vim 
" Using vim-plug
call plug#begin()
  Plug 'hashivim/vim-terraform'
  Plug 'juliosueiras/vim-terraform-completion'
call plug#end()

" Terraform settings
let g:terraform_align=1
let g:terraform_fmt_on_save=1
let g:terraform_fold_sections=1

" Auto-format on save
autocmd BufWritePre *.tf lua vim.lsp.buf.formatting_sync()
Lua Neovim LSP Setup (init.lua) 
-- Using nvim-lspconfig for Terraform LSP
require'lspconfig'.terraformls.setup{}

-- Auto-format on save
vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd({"BufWritePre"}, {
  pattern = {"*.tf", "*.tfvars"},
  callback = function()
    vim.lsp.buf.format()
  end,
})
🔗

2.3.5 Terraform Language Server (LSP)

🔗 Language Server Protocol

The Terraform Language Server provides IDE features to any editor that supports LSP.

⬇️ Installing terraform-ls
Bash Install Terraform Language Server 
# macOS
brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform-ls

# Linux (Ubuntu/Debian) - Add HashiCorp repo first
sudo apt-get install terraform-ls

# Windows (Chocolatey)
choco install terraform-ls

# Verify
terraform-ls --version
LSP Features:
  • Completion: Context-aware auto-completion
  • Hover: Documentation on hover
  • Formatting: Format documents and selections
  • Validation: Real-time syntax validation
  • Navigation: Go to definition, find references
  • Symbols: Document and workspace symbols

2.4 AWS CLI Configuration

HANDS-ON ☁️ AWS
🎯

What You're Doing Here

Terraform needs to authenticate with AWS to create resources. The most common way to do this is through the AWS CLI. In this section, you'll install the AWS CLI and configure credentials that Terraform will automatically use. You'll also learn to set up multiple profiles for different AWS accounts.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Have AWS CLI installed and configured
  • Understand how to manage multiple AWS profiles
  • Be ready for Terraform to authenticate with AWS
☁️

🔧 AWS CLI - Your Gateway to AWS

The AWS CLI is essential for Terraform AWS provider authentication and for debugging AWS resources outside of Terraform.

  • Required for AWS provider authentication
  • Configure multiple profiles for different accounts
  • Support for SSO and assumed roles
  • Essential debugging tool alongside Terraform
v2 Current Version
~/.aws/ Config Location
∞ Named Profiles
SSO Modern Auth
KEYS
🔑

Access Keys

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID + AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY. Long-term credentials stored in ~/.aws/credentials.

PROFILE
👤

Named Profiles

Multiple credential sets for different accounts. Switch with AWS_PROFILE or --profile flag.

SSO
🔗

AWS SSO

Modern auth via browser login. Short-lived tokens. Ideal for enterprise environments.

VERIFY
✅

sts get-caller-identity

Verify which AWS account and identity you're authenticated as. Essential debugging command.

⬇️

2.4.1 Installing AWS CLI

🪟 Windows Installation
PowerShell Install AWS CLI v2 
# Download and run MSI installer
msiexec.exe /i https://awscli.amazonaws.com/AWSCLIV2.msi

# Or via Chocolatey
choco install awscli

# Verify installation (open new terminal)
aws --version
🍎 macOS Installation
Bash Install AWS CLI v2 
# Using Homebrew (Recommended)
brew install awscli

# Or official installer
curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/AWSCLIV2.pkg" -o "AWSCLIV2.pkg"
sudo installer -pkg AWSCLIV2.pkg -target /

# Verify
aws --version
🐧 Linux Installation
Bash Install AWS CLI v2 
# Download and install
curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
unzip awscliv2.zip
sudo ./aws/install

# For ARM64 (e.g., Graviton)
curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-aarch64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
unzip awscliv2.zip
sudo ./aws/install

# Verify
aws --version

# Cleanup
rm -rf awscliv2.zip aws/
Expected Output
aws-cli/2.15.30 Python/3.11.8 Linux/5.15.0-1053-aws exe/x86_64.ubuntu.22 prompt/off
⚙️

2.4.2 AWS Configure

🔐 Initial Configuration

Configure your AWS credentials using aws configure which creates files in ~/.aws/

🔑 Basic Configuration
Bash aws configure 
# Run interactive configuration
aws configure

# You'll be prompted for:
# AWS Access Key ID [None]: AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
# AWS Secret Access Key [None]: wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY
# Default region name [None]: us-east-1
# Default output format [None]: json
📁 AWS Configuration Files
~/.aws/
├── credentials          # Access keys (NEVER commit this!)
│   └── [default]
│       aws_access_key_id = AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
│       aws_secret_access_key = wJalrXUtnF...
│
└── config              # Region and output preferences
    └── [default]
        region = us-east-1
        output = json
👤

2.4.3 Named Profiles

👥 Multiple AWS Accounts

Use named profiles to manage credentials for multiple AWS accounts or roles.

📝 Creating Named Profiles
Bash Configure Named Profiles 
# Create profile for development account
aws configure --profile dev

# Create profile for production account
aws configure --profile prod

# Create profile for staging
aws configure --profile staging

# Test profile
aws sts get-caller-identity --profile dev
INI ~/.aws/credentials 
[default]
aws_access_key_id = AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
aws_secret_access_key = wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY

[dev]
aws_access_key_id = AKIAI44QH8DHBEXAMPLE
aws_secret_access_key = je7MtGbClwBF/2Zp9Utk/h3yCo8nvbEXAMPLEKEY

[prod]
aws_access_key_id = AKIAI33AABBCCDDEEFFF
aws_secret_access_key = xyzABC123/MNO456/pqr789EXAMPLEKEY
INI ~/.aws/config 
[default]
region = us-east-1
output = json

[profile dev]
region = us-west-2
output = json

[profile prod]
region = us-east-1
output = json
🔄 Using Profiles with Terraform
HCL Specify Profile in Provider 
# Method 1: Hardcode profile (not recommended for shared code)
provider "aws" {
  region  = "us-east-1"
  profile = "dev"
}

# Method 2: Use variable (better)
variable "aws_profile" {
  default = "default"
}

provider "aws" {
  region  = "us-east-1"
  profile = var.aws_profile
}
Bash Using Environment Variable 
# Set profile via environment variable
export AWS_PROFILE=dev
terraform plan

# Or inline
AWS_PROFILE=prod terraform apply

# Or pass as variable
terraform apply -var="aws_profile=prod"
✅

2.4.4 Verify Configuration

🔍 Verification Commands
Bash Verify AWS CLI Setup 
# Check current identity
aws sts get-caller-identity

# Check with specific profile
aws sts get-caller-identity --profile dev

# List configured profiles
aws configure list-profiles

# Show current configuration
aws configure list
aws sts get-caller-identity
{
    "UserId": "AIDAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE",
    "Account": "123456789012",
    "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/terraform-user"
}
aws configure list
      Name                    Value             Type    Location
      ----                    -----             ----    --------
   profile                <not set>             None    None
access_key     ****************MPLE shared-credentials-file    
secret_key     ****************EKEY shared-credentials-file    
    region                us-east-1      config-file    ~/.aws/config
⚠️
SECURITY: Never commit ~/.aws/credentials or hardcode credentials in Terraform files. Use environment variables, IAM roles, or AWS SSO in production.

2.5 Authentication Methods

📝 EXAM TOPIC 🔐 SECURITY
🎯

Why This Section Matters

Authentication is the most critical security aspect of your Terraform setup. How Terraform proves its identity to AWS determines who can make changes to your infrastructure and what audit trail is left behind. Getting this wrong can expose your entire AWS account to attackers, while getting it right creates a secure, traceable, and compliant infrastructure management process. This section teaches you all the authentication methods Terraform supports for AWS, helping you choose the right one for your specific environment.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Understand why hardcoded credentials are dangerous and should never be used
  • Know how to use environment variables for CI/CD pipelines and local development
  • Be able to configure shared credentials files for multi-profile AWS management
  • Understand IAM instance roles and why they're the gold standard for EC2-hosted Terraform
  • Learn how to assume roles for cross-account access and temporary credentials
  • Know the authentication precedence order Terraform uses to find credentials
🔐

🎯 Secure Authentication

Terraform supports multiple authentication methods for AWS. Choosing the right method depends on your environment and security requirements.

  • Environment Variables - Simple, good for CI/CD
  • Shared Credentials - ~/.aws/credentials file
  • IAM Roles - Best for EC2/ECS/Lambda
  • AWS SSO - Modern approach for organizations
7 Auth Methods
IAM Best for EC2
SSO Best for Teams
ENV Best for CI/CD
AVOID
❌

Hardcoded Keys

Never put credentials in code. Exposed in version control.

CI/CD
🔑

Environment Vars

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID + AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY. Good for pipelines.

LOCAL
📁

Shared Credentials

~/.aws/credentials file. Supports multiple profiles.

AWS
🛡️

IAM Instance Roles

Automatic credentials for EC2/ECS/Lambda. Most secure.

TEAMS
👥

AWS SSO

Modern approach. Short-lived tokens with federated login.

CROSS
🔄

Assume Role

Cross-account access with temporary credentials.

📊 AWS Credential Precedence (Highest to Lowest) 
 ╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
 ║                  AWS CREDENTIAL RESOLUTION ORDER                           ║
 ╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
 ║                                                                           ║
 ║   🔝 HIGHEST PRIORITY                                                     ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  1. Provider block credentials (hardcoded - AVOID!)               │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  2. Environment variables                                         │   ║
 ║   │     AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID + AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY                     │   ║
 ║   │     AWS_PROFILE (to select shared credentials profile)            │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  3. Shared credentials file                                       │   ║
 ║   │     ~/.aws/credentials (profile from provider or AWS_PROFILE)     │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  4. AWS Config/SSO Profile (~/.aws/config)                        │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  5. Container credentials (ECS task role)                         │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  6. Instance profile (EC2 IAM role via metadata service)          │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║   🔻 LOWEST PRIORITY                                                      ║
 ║                                                                           ║
 ╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
STS Assumed Roles
🔑

2.5.1 Static Credentials (Not Recommended)

⚠️
WARNING: Hardcoding credentials in Terraform files is a security risk. Use this only for learning or quick tests, NEVER in production.
❌ Hardcoded Credentials (AVOID)
HCL ❌ DO NOT DO THIS 
# ❌ NEVER DO THIS - Credentials in plain text!
provider "aws" {
  region     = "us-east-1"
  access_key = "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
  secret_key = "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
}

# If committed to Git, credentials are exposed forever
# Use environment variables or IAM roles instead!
🌍

2.5.2 Environment Variables

✅ Good for CI/CD

Environment variables are a safe way to pass credentials without putting them in code.

🌍 AWS Environment Variables
Bash Export AWS Credentials 
# Set credentials
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION="us-east-1"

# For session tokens (temporary credentials)
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN="FwoGZXIvYXdzEBY..."

# Now run Terraform - it will use these automatically
terraform init
terraform plan
HCL Provider with Env Variables 
# No credentials in code - uses environment variables
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  # AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, 
  # AWS_REGION are automatically detected
}
📄

2.5.3 Shared Credentials File

📄 Using ~/.aws/credentials
HCL Provider with Profile 
# Use default profile
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  # Automatically uses [default] from ~/.aws/credentials
}

# Use named profile
provider "aws" {
  region                  = "us-east-1"
  profile                 = "dev"
  shared_credentials_files = ["~/.aws/credentials"]  # Optional, this is default
}

# Custom credentials file location
provider "aws" {
  region                  = "us-east-1"
  shared_credentials_files = ["/path/to/custom/credentials"]
}
🏷️

2.5.4 IAM Instance Roles (Best Practice)

⭐ Recommended for EC2/ECS

IAM roles provide automatic credential rotation with no secrets to manage.

🏷️ Using IAM Instance Profile
HCL Provider on EC2 with IAM Role 
# When running on EC2 with an IAM role attached,
# credentials are automatic via instance metadata
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  # No credentials needed! Uses instance profile automatically
}

# For ECS tasks, use task execution role
# For Lambda, use function execution role
# Terraform detects and uses the role automatically
🔄
Auto-Rotation

Credentials rotate automatically

🔒
No Secrets

No credentials to store or manage

📊
Auditable

CloudTrail tracks all API calls

⚡
Zero Config

Works automatically on EC2/ECS/Lambda

🎭

2.5.5 Assume Role (Cross-Account)

🎭 Assuming IAM Roles
HCL Assume Role in Provider 
# Assume a role in another account
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_ID:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "terraform-session"
    external_id  = "my-external-id"  # Optional, for cross-account security
  }
}

# Multi-account setup with provider aliases
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "production"
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "development"
  region = "us-west-2"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}
🔗

2.5.6 AWS SSO (IAM Identity Center)

🏢 Enterprise Authentication

AWS SSO (now IAM Identity Center) provides centralized access management for organizations.

🔗 Configuring AWS SSO
Bash AWS SSO Login 
# Configure SSO profile
aws configure sso --profile my-sso-profile

# You'll be prompted for:
# SSO session name: my-sso
# SSO start URL: https://my-company.awsapps.com/start
# SSO region: us-east-1

# Login via browser
aws sso login --profile my-sso-profile

# Use with Terraform
export AWS_PROFILE=my-sso-profile
terraform plan
INI ~/.aws/config SSO Profile 
[profile my-sso-profile]
sso_session = my-sso
sso_account_id = 123456789012
sso_role_name = TerraformRole
region = us-east-1
output = json

[sso-session my-sso]
sso_start_url = https://my-company.awsapps.com/start
sso_region = us-east-1
sso_registration_scopes = sso:account:access
📊

2.5.7 Authentication Priority Order

📊 Credential Resolution Order

The AWS provider checks for credentials in this order:

1️⃣ Provider Block

Static credentials in the provider (not recommended)

2️⃣ Environment Variables

AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

3️⃣ Shared Credentials File

~/.aws/credentials (default or named profile)

4️⃣ Shared Config File

~/.aws/config with credential_process

5️⃣ Container Credentials

ECS task role via container agent

6️⃣ Instance Metadata

EC2 instance profile (IMDS)

📝
EXAM TIP: Know the credential resolution order. The exam may ask which credentials take precedence when multiple are configured. Environment variables override shared credentials.

2.6 Environment Variables

📝 EXAM TOPIC REFERENCE
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning how to control Terraform's behavior without modifying configuration files. Environment variables are the primary way to configure Terraform in CI/CD pipelines, set debugging options, pass sensitive values, and customize Terraform's runtime behavior. Understanding these variables is essential for automation, troubleshooting production issues, and building portable infrastructure code that works across different environments.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know how to pass variable values using TF_VAR_* environment variables
  • Be able to enable and configure Terraform logging with TF_LOG and TF_LOG_PATH
  • Understand how to set up plugin caching with TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR
  • Know how to pass default CLI arguments with TF_CLI_ARGS
  • Be able to control Terraform's input/output behavior for automation
  • Understand all key TF_* variables tested on the certification exam
🌍

⚙️ Terraform Environment Variables

Terraform recognizes several environment variables that configure its behavior. These are useful for automation, debugging, and CI/CD pipelines.

  • TF_* variables control Terraform behavior
  • TF_VAR_* sets input variable values
  • TF_LOG for debugging output
  • Essential for CI/CD configuration
8+ TF_* Variables
5 Log Levels
TF_VAR_* Variable Pattern
CI/CD Primary Use
VARS
📝

TF_VAR_*

Set input variable values. TF_VAR_name=value replaces variables.tf defaults.

DEBUG
🔍

TF_LOG

Set log verbosity: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR. Essential for troubleshooting.

LOGS
📁

TF_LOG_PATH

Save logs to file instead of stderr. Required for CI/CD pipelines.

CACHE
💾

TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR

Cache providers across projects. Speeds up terraform init significantly.

CLI
⚙️

TF_CLI_ARGS

Default CLI arguments. TF_CLI_ARGS_apply for command-specific args.

AUTO
🤖

TF_INPUT

Disable interactive prompts. Set to 0 for non-interactive automation.

📝

2.6.1 TF_VAR_ Prefix

📝 Setting Variable Values

Use TF_VAR_<name> to set Terraform input variable values via environment variables.

📝 Variable Environment Variables
HCL variables.tf 
# Define variables in Terraform
variable "region" {
  type    = string
  default = "us-east-1"
}

variable "instance_type" {
  type = string
}

variable "environment" {
  type = string
}
Bash Setting TF_VAR_ Variables 
# Set variable values via environment
export TF_VAR_region="us-west-2"
export TF_VAR_instance_type="t3.micro"
export TF_VAR_environment="production"

# Now terraform will use these values
terraform plan

# Or inline for one-time use
TF_VAR_environment=staging terraform apply
💡
TIP: Variable names are case-sensitive. TF_VAR_Region is different from TF_VAR_region. Use lowercase to match typical Terraform naming conventions.
📋

2.6.2 TF_LOG (Logging)

🔍 Debugging Terraform

TF_LOG controls the verbosity of Terraform's logging output for debugging.

📋 Log Levels
Bash TF_LOG Levels 
# Log levels (from least to most verbose)
export TF_LOG=ERROR   # Only errors
export TF_LOG=WARN    # Warnings and errors
export TF_LOG=INFO    # Informational messages
export TF_LOG=DEBUG   # Debugging information
export TF_LOG=TRACE   # Most detailed (includes API calls)

# Common debugging usage
TF_LOG=DEBUG terraform plan

# Save logs to file
export TF_LOG=DEBUG
export TF_LOG_PATH="./terraform.log"
terraform plan

# Disable logging
unset TF_LOG
Level Shows Use Case
ERROR Critical errors only Production, quiet mode
WARN Warnings + errors Production with warnings
INFO General information Understanding flow
DEBUG Debug details Troubleshooting
TRACE Everything (API calls) Deep debugging
📁

2.6.3 TF_LOG_PATH

📁 Log File Output
Bash Saving Logs to File 
# Set log level and output path
export TF_LOG=TRACE
export TF_LOG_PATH="./terraform-debug.log"

# Run terraform - logs go to file instead of stderr
terraform plan

# View logs
cat terraform-debug.log

# For CI/CD - timestamped logs
export TF_LOG_PATH="/var/log/terraform/terraform-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).log"
📂

2.6.4 TF_DATA_DIR

📂 Custom Data Directory
Bash TF_DATA_DIR Usage 
# Default location is .terraform in current directory
# Change to custom location
export TF_DATA_DIR="/opt/terraform-data"

# Useful for:
# - Shared plugin cache
# - CI/CD caching
# - Centralized provider storage

terraform init  # Plugins go to TF_DATA_DIR instead of .terraform/
💾

2.6.5 TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR

⚡ Speed Up terraform init

Cache downloaded providers to avoid re-downloading for every project.

💾 Provider Plugin Caching
Bash Plugin Cache Setup 
# Set plugin cache directory
export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"

# Create the directory
mkdir -p $TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR

# Add to shell profile for persistence
echo 'export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"' >> ~/.bashrc

# Now providers are cached and reused across projects
# First init downloads provider
cd project-1 && terraform init

# Second project reuses cached provider
cd ../project-2 && terraform init  # Much faster!
⚡
Faster Init

Skip downloads for cached providers

💿
Save Bandwidth

Download each provider version once

🏗️
CI/CD Friendly

Cache between pipeline runs

🔧

2.6.6 TF_CLI_ARGS

🔧 Default CLI Arguments
Bash TF_CLI_ARGS Examples 
# Add default arguments to ALL terraform commands
export TF_CLI_ARGS="-input=false"

# Add arguments to specific commands
export TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-out=tfplan -detailed-exitcode"
export TF_CLI_ARGS_apply="-auto-approve"
export TF_CLI_ARGS_init="-upgrade"

# Example: Auto-approve for CI/CD
export TF_CLI_ARGS_apply="-auto-approve"
terraform apply  # Runs as: terraform apply -auto-approve

# Combine multiple arguments
export TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-out=plan.tfplan -parallelism=20"
⌨️

2.6.7 TF_INPUT

⌨️ Disable Interactive Input
Bash TF_INPUT for Non-Interactive 
# Disable interactive prompts (for CI/CD)
export TF_INPUT=0

# or
export TF_INPUT=false

# Terraform will fail instead of prompting for missing variables
# Useful in automation to catch configuration errors
📊

2.6.8 Environment Variables Summary

📊 Quick Reference Table
Variable Purpose Example
TF_VAR_* Set input variables TF_VAR_region=us-east-1
TF_LOG Set log level TF_LOG=DEBUG
TF_LOG_PATH Log file location TF_LOG_PATH=./tf.log
TF_DATA_DIR Data directory location TF_DATA_DIR=/opt/tf
TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR Provider cache TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR=~/.terraform.d/cache
TF_CLI_ARGS Default CLI args TF_CLI_ARGS_apply=-auto-approve
TF_INPUT Disable prompts TF_INPUT=0
TF_IN_AUTOMATION Automation mode TF_IN_AUTOMATION=1
📝
EXAM TIP: Know the common environment variables, especially TF_VAR_*, TF_LOG, and TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR. Questions may ask how to set variable values or debug Terraform.

2.7 Project Directory Structure

BEST PRACTICE
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning how to organize your Terraform code like a professional. Unlike some tools with rigid folder structures, Terraform is flexible—it processes all .tf files in a directory regardless of how you name them. This freedom is powerful but can lead to chaos without conventions. This section teaches you industry-standard patterns for structuring projects that are easy to navigate, maintain, collaborate on, and scale from single-developer projects to enterprise multi-environment deployments.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know the standard Terraform file naming conventions (main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, etc.)
  • Understand when and how to split configuration across multiple files
  • Be able to structure multi-environment projects (dev/staging/prod)
  • Know what files to include in .gitignore for Terraform projects
  • Understand how to organize modules for maximum reusability
  • Be able to set up a production-ready project structure from scratch
📁

🏗️ Organizing Your Terraform Code

A well-organized directory structure makes your Terraform code maintainable, reusable, and easier to work with in teams.

  • Consistent file naming conventions
  • Logical separation of concerns
  • Module structure for reusability
  • Environment organization patterns
6 Core Files
3+ Environments
∞ Modules
1 State per Env
MAIN
📄

main.tf

Primary configuration with resources, data sources, and module calls.

INPUT
📥

variables.tf

Input variable declarations with types, defaults, and descriptions.

OUTPUT
📤

outputs.tf

Export values for display, modules, or external tools.

PROVIDER
☁️

providers.tf

Provider configuration blocks and aliases for multi-region.

VERSION
🔢

versions.tf

Required Terraform and provider version constraints.

VALUES
⚙️

terraform.tfvars

Variable values for the current environment. Keep secrets out!

📄

2.7.1 Standard File Layout

📄 Common File Naming
📁 Minimal Project Structure
my-terraform-project/
├── main.tf              # Main resource definitions
├── variables.tf         # Input variable declarations
├── outputs.tf           # Output value definitions
├── providers.tf         # Provider configuration
├── versions.tf          # Required versions constraints
├── terraform.tfvars     # Variable values (gitignored)
├── .gitignore           # Git ignore patterns
└── README.md            # Project documentation
File Purpose Contents
main.tf Primary configuration Resources, data sources, modules
variables.tf Input definitions variable blocks with descriptions
outputs.tf Output definitions output blocks to expose values
providers.tf Provider setup provider blocks, aliases
versions.tf Version constraints terraform and provider versions
terraform.tfvars Variable values Actual values (don't commit secrets)
⭐

2.7.2 Recommended File Structure

⭐ Production-Ready Layout
📁 Comprehensive Project Structure
terraform-aws-infrastructure/
├── main.tf                      # Root module, calls child modules
├── variables.tf                 # All input variables
├── outputs.tf                   # All outputs
├── versions.tf                  # Terraform & provider versions
├── providers.tf                 # Provider configurations
├── locals.tf                    # Local values and computed values
├── data.tf                      # Data sources
├── backend.tf                   # Backend configuration
│
├── terraform.tfvars             # Default variable values
├── dev.tfvars                   # Development environment values
├── staging.tfvars               # Staging environment values
├── prod.tfvars                  # Production environment values
│
├── modules/                     # Local/custom modules
│   ├── vpc/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   ├── ec2/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   └── rds/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       └── outputs.tf
│
├── scripts/                     # Helper scripts
│   ├── deploy.sh
│   └── destroy.sh
│
├── .gitignore                   # Git ignore patterns
├── .terraform-version           # tfenv version pinning
├── .pre-commit-config.yaml      # Pre-commit hooks
└── README.md                    # Documentation
🌍

2.7.3 Multi-Environment Layout

🌍 Environment Directories
📁 Environment-Based Structure
infrastructure/
├── environments/
│   ├── dev/
│   │   ├── main.tf              # Uses modules with dev settings
│   │   ├── backend.tf           # Dev state backend
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars     # Dev variable values
│   │   └── .terraform-version   # Version for dev
│   │
│   ├── staging/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── backend.tf           # Staging state backend
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars
│   │   └── .terraform-version
│   │
│   └── prod/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── backend.tf           # Prod state backend (S3+DynamoDB)
│       ├── terraform.tfvars
│       └── .terraform-version
│
└── modules/                     # Shared modules
    ├── networking/
    ├── compute/
    ├── database/
    └── security/
Bash Working with Environments 
# Deploy to development
cd environments/dev
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply

# Deploy to production (separate state!)
cd ../prod
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply
🚫

2.7.4 .gitignore for Terraform

🚫 Essential .gitignore
Gitignore .gitignore 
# .gitignore for Terraform projects

# Terraform directories
.terraform/
.terraform.lock.hcl

# State files (DO NOT COMMIT!)
*.tfstate
*.tfstate.*
*.tfstate.backup

# Plan files
*.tfplan
*plan*

# Variable files with secrets
*.tfvars
!example.tfvars
terraform.tfvars.json

# Crash logs
crash.log
crash.*.log

# Override files
override.tf
override.tf.json
*_override.tf
*_override.tf.json

# CLI config
.terraformrc
terraform.rc

# IDE
.idea/
*.swp
*.swo
.vscode/

# OS files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Logs
*.log
⚠️
CRITICAL: Never commit terraform.tfstate, *.tfvars with secrets, or .terraform/ directory to version control. Use remote state backends instead.
✨

2.7.5 File Organization Best Practices

📝
Descriptive Names

Use ec2.tf, rds.tf for large projects instead of one huge main.tf

📦
Modularize Early

Extract reusable components into modules before they become complex

📖
Document Everything

Include README.md with usage examples and variable descriptions

🔒
Separate Secrets

Keep sensitive values in secure backends, not in files

📂 Large Project Flat Structure
📁 Resource-Based File Organization
aws-infrastructure/
├── versions.tf          # Terraform version constraints
├── providers.tf         # AWS provider(s) configuration
├── backend.tf           # S3 remote state configuration
├── variables.tf         # All input variables
├── outputs.tf           # All outputs
├── locals.tf            # Local values
├── data.tf              # All data sources
│
├── vpc.tf               # VPC, subnets, route tables, NAT
├── security-groups.tf   # All security groups
├── ec2.tf               # EC2 instances
├── alb.tf               # Application Load Balancer
├── rds.tf               # RDS databases
├── s3.tf                # S3 buckets
├── iam.tf               # IAM roles and policies
├── cloudwatch.tf        # CloudWatch alarms and logs
└── route53.tf           # DNS records

2.8 Git Configuration for Terraform

BEST PRACTICE
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're setting up professional-grade version control practices for infrastructure code. Unlike application code where a bug might break a feature, a Terraform bug can accidentally delete databases or expose secrets. This section teaches you how to configure Git hooks that automatically validate formatting, catch security issues, and enforce code quality before infrastructure changes ever reach your main branch. These practices are essential for team environments and are expected in any professional Terraform workflow.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know how to install and configure pre-commit hooks for Terraform
  • Be able to set up TFLint for linting and catching common mistakes
  • Understand how to run security scanning with Trivy or tfsec
  • Know how to auto-generate documentation with terraform-docs
  • Be able to set up a complete code quality pipeline before commits
  • Understand CI/CD integration patterns for Terraform validation
📚

🔄 Version Control Best Practices

Proper Git configuration is essential for collaborative Terraform development, including hooks for validation and consistent formatting.

  • Pre-commit hooks for validation
  • Branch protection for infrastructure changes
  • Consistent commit message conventions
  • Pull request workflows
🪝

2.8.1 Pre-commit Hooks

🛡️ Catch Errors Before Commit

Pre-commit hooks automatically validate and format your Terraform code before each commit.

⬇️ Installing pre-commit
Bash Install pre-commit Framework 
# Install pre-commit (Python package)
pip install pre-commit

# Or via Homebrew (macOS)
brew install pre-commit

# Verify
pre-commit --version
📝 Terraform Pre-commit Config
YAML .pre-commit-config.yaml 
repos:
  # Terraform formatting and validation
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.86.0
    hooks:
      - id: terraform_fmt
      - id: terraform_validate
      - id: terraform_docs
        args:
          - --hook-config=--path-to-file=README.md
          - --hook-config=--add-to-existing-file=true
          - --hook-config=--create-file-if-not-exist=true
      - id: terraform_tflint
        args:
          - --args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.tflint.hcl
      - id: terraform_trivy
        args:
          - --args=--severity=HIGH,CRITICAL
      
  # General file checks
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-yaml
      - id: check-json
      - id: check-merge-conflict
      - id: detect-aws-credentials
      - id: detect-private-key
      - id: no-commit-to-branch
        args: [--branch, main, --branch, master]
Bash Initialize pre-commit 
# Install the git hooks
pre-commit install

# Run manually on all files
pre-commit run --all-files

# Skip hooks for emergency commits (not recommended)
git commit --no-verify -m "Emergency fix"
🔍

2.8.2 TFLint Configuration

🔍 TFLint Setup
Bash Install TFLint 
# macOS
brew install tflint

# Linux
curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/terraform-linters/tflint/master/install_linux.sh | bash

# Windows
choco install tflint

# Verify
tflint --version
HCL .tflint.hcl 
# TFLint configuration
config {
  plugin_dir = "~/.tflint.d/plugins"
  
  module = true
}

# AWS Provider Plugin
plugin "aws" {
  enabled = true
  version = "0.29.0"
  source  = "github.com/terraform-linters/tflint-ruleset-aws"
}

# Terraform rules
plugin "terraform" {
  enabled = true
  preset  = "recommended"
}

# Custom rules
rule "terraform_naming_convention" {
  enabled = true
  format  = "snake_case"
}

rule "terraform_documented_outputs" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_documented_variables" {
  enabled = true
}
💬

2.8.3 Commit Message Conventions

💬 Conventional Commits
Text Commit Message Format 
# Format: <type>(<scope>): <description>

# Types for Terraform:
feat(vpc): add private subnets for database tier
fix(ec2): correct security group ingress rules
refactor(modules): extract RDS into separate module
docs(readme): add deployment instructions
chore(deps): update AWS provider to 5.30
test(vpc): add validation for CIDR blocks
ci(github): add terraform plan workflow

# Examples:
feat(rds): add multi-az support for production database
fix(iam): restrict S3 bucket policy permissions
refactor(networking): consolidate route tables
style: format all tf files with terraform fmt
docs(modules): add usage examples for vpc module
🌳

2.8.4 Branch Strategy for Infrastructure

🌳 Recommended Branch Strategy
🌳 Git Flow for Terraform
main (production)
    │
    ├─── develop (staging/testing)
    │       │
    │       ├─── feature/add-rds-cluster
    │       │       └── terraform plan → PR to develop
    │       │
    │       ├─── feature/update-vpc-cidr
    │       │       └── terraform plan → PR to develop
    │       │
    │       └─── hotfix/fix-security-group
    │               └── terraform plan → PR to main (urgent)
    │
    └─── releases/v1.2.0
            └── tag and deploy to production
Branch Protection Rules:
  • main: Require PR reviews, terraform plan must pass
  • develop: Require successful terraform validate
  • features: Run terraform plan on every push
  • No direct commits: All changes through PRs
💡
TIP: Always run terraform plan in your PR pipeline and post the plan output as a PR comment. This allows reviewers to see exactly what changes will be applied.

📌 Chapter 2 Summary

Key Takeaways
  • Installation: Single binary, available via package managers or direct download
  • tfenv: Version manager for multiple Terraform versions per project
  • IDE Setup: VS Code with HashiCorp extension for best experience
  • AWS CLI: Configure profiles for different accounts/environments
  • Authentication: Use IAM roles (EC2) or environment variables (CI/CD)
  • Environment Variables: TF_VAR_*, TF_LOG, TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR
  • Project Structure: Consistent file naming and organization
  • Git Setup: Pre-commit hooks, .gitignore, branching strategy
Exam Focus Points
  • ✅ terraform version command
  • ✅ AWS authentication methods and priority order
  • ✅ TF_VAR_* environment variables for input values
  • ✅ TF_LOG levels for debugging
  • ✅ Which files should NOT be committed (.tfstate, secrets)
  • ✅ terraform init downloads providers to .terraform/
← Previous: Chapter 1 - Introduction Next: Chapter 3 - HCL Deep Dive →
📝
🔤
💡
⚡

03 HCL Deep Dive

Master the HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)—the expressive, human-readable syntax that powers every Terraform configuration. This comprehensive deep dive takes you from basic syntax rules through advanced language features, ensuring you can read, write, and debug any Terraform code with confidence. You'll learn how blocks, arguments, and expressions work together to define infrastructure, master all data types from simple strings to complex nested objects, and unlock the full power of Terraform's 100+ built-in functions. Whether you're defining a single variable or writing dynamic blocks that generate configuration programmatically, this chapter gives you complete command of the language.

🎯 Core Knowledge 📝 High Exam Weight ⏱️ 45 Minutes
📚 7 Sections
💡 30+ Subsections
📝 High Exam Weight
🔧 50+ Functions

Chapter 3 HCL Deep Dive

PART 1 INTERMEDIATE Difficulty: ★★★☆☆

HCL is the language you'll write every day as a Terraform practitioner, and this chapter ensures you master it completely. We start with foundational syntax—understanding how blocks are structured with types and labels, how arguments accept values of different types, and how expressions let you reference other resources and compute values dynamically. You'll explore Terraform's rich type system including primitives (string, number, bool), collections (list, map, set), and structural types (object, tuple) with real examples of when to use each. The variables section covers input validation, sensitive values, and best practices for creating flexible, reusable configurations. We dive deep into expressions—operators, conditionals, for loops, and splat expressions that let you manipulate data elegantly. The built-in functions library (string manipulation, collection operations, encoding, filesystem, date/time) becomes your power toolkit. Finally, you'll master dynamic blocks for generating repetitive configuration programmatically—essential for security groups, IAM policies, and more.

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Master HCL Syntax Fundamentals — Understand block structure (type, labels, body), argument assignment, identifier naming rules, comments (# and /* */), and how HCL relates to JSON for programmatic configuration
  • ✅ Work with All Terraform Data Types — Master primitives (string, number, bool), collections (list, map, set with type constraints), and structural types (object, tuple) with practical examples and type conversion
  • ✅ Define Powerful Input Variables — Create variables with descriptions, defaults, type constraints, validation rules, and sensitive flags; understand variable precedence (CLI, env, files, defaults)
  • ✅ Create Outputs and Local Values — Export values for other configurations or human consumption, create computed local values to simplify expressions and avoid repetition
  • ✅ Write Advanced Expressions — Use arithmetic/comparison/logical operators, conditional expressions (ternary), for expressions to transform collections, and splat expressions ([*]) for list operations
  • ✅ Leverage 100+ Built-in Functions — Master string functions (format, join, split, regex), collection functions (merge, lookup, flatten), encoding functions (jsonencode, base64), type conversions, and more
  • ✅ Implement Dynamic Blocks — Generate repetitive nested blocks programmatically using dynamic with for_each, essential for security group rules, IAM policies, and any repeated configuration patterns

📑 In This Chapter

3.1 HCL Syntax Fundamentals 3.2 Variables and Types 3.3 Outputs 3.4 Local Values 3.5 Data Sources 3.6 Expressions and Operators 3.7 Functions Overview
💻

Language Fundamentals

📚 7 Sections
⏱️ 45 min read
📝 High Exam Weight
📝

Syntax Fundamentals

Master blocks, arguments, identifiers, and expressions—the building blocks of every configuration

📊

Variables & Types

Define input variables with type constraints, validation rules, and understand Terraform's type system

📤

Outputs & Locals

Export values for other configurations and create computed local values to simplify your code

🔧

Built-in Functions

100+ functions for strings, collections, encoding, filesystem operations, and data transformation

3.1 HCL Syntax Fundamentals

CORE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning the grammar and syntax of Terraform's configuration language. HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) is the foundation of everything you write in Terraform. Understanding its structure—blocks, arguments, identifiers, and expressions—is like learning the alphabet before writing sentences. Every resource you create, every variable you define, and every module you call follows these same syntactic patterns. Master this section and you'll read and write Terraform code fluently.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Understand the anatomy of HCL blocks (type, labels, body)
  • Know how to write and assign arguments with different value types
  • Understand identifier naming rules and conventions
  • Know how to write comments (single-line and multi-line)
  • Understand HCL's relationship to JSON
  • Be able to read any Terraform configuration and understand its structure
📝

🔤 HashiCorp Configuration Language

HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) is a structured configuration language designed to be both human-readable and machine-friendly. It strikes a balance between JSON and more complex programming languages.

  • Declarative syntax - describe desired state
  • Human-readable with clear structure
  • Supports expressions and functions
  • Native JSON interoperability
8 Block Types
6 Primitive Types
100+ Built-in Functions
∞ Expressions
CORE
🧱

Blocks

Container units with type, labels, and body. Resources, variables, outputs are all blocks.

VALUE
📝

Arguments

Key-value pairs inside blocks that configure attributes. Use = for assignment.

NAME
🏷️

Identifiers

Names for resources, variables, outputs. Use snake_case convention.

DYNAMIC
⚡

Expressions

Dynamic values using references, functions, operators, and conditionals.

🧱

3.1.1 Blocks

🧱 Building Blocks of HCL

Blocks are containers for configuration. Each block has a type, optional labels, and a body containing arguments.

🧱 Block Structure
HCL Block Anatomy 
# Block structure:
# block_type "label1" "label2" {
#   argument = value
#   nested_block {
#     ...
#   }
# }

# Resource block: type "resource_type" "local_name"
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "WebServer"
  }
}

# Variable block: type "variable_name"
variable "region" {
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
  description = "AWS region"
}

# Provider block: type "provider_name"
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}
📐 Block Anatomy Diagram
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  resource "aws_instance" "web" {                            │
│  ────────  ─────────────  ───                               │
│  Block     Label 1        Label 2                           │
│  Type      (Resource      (Local                            │
│            Type)          Name)                             │
│                                                             │
│    ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"                  │
│    ───             ──────────────────────                   │
│    Argument        Value (string)                           │
│    Name                                                     │
│                                                             │
│    tags = {                     ← Nested block /            │
│      Name = "WebServer"           argument with object      │
│    }                                                        │
│  }                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📋 Common Block Types
Block Type Labels Purpose
resource type, name Define infrastructure resources
data type, name Query existing resources
variable name Input parameters
output name Export values
locals (none) Local computed values
module name Call child modules
provider name Configure providers
terraform (none) Terraform settings
📝

3.1.2 Arguments

📝 Arguments and Values
HCL Argument Examples 
# Arguments assign values to names
resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  # Simple string argument
  ami = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  
  # Number argument  
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  
  # Boolean argument
  associate_public_ip_address = true
  
  # List argument
  security_groups = ["sg-abc123", "sg-def456"]
  
  # Map/object argument
  tags = {
    Name        = "MyInstance"
    Environment = "Production"
  }
  
  # Reference to another resource
  subnet_id = aws_subnet.main.id
  
  # Expression with function
  user_data = file("startup.sh")
}
🏷️

3.1.3 Identifiers

🏷️ Naming Rules
HCL Valid and Invalid Identifiers 
# Valid identifiers:
variable "my_variable" {}      # letters, numbers, underscores
variable "myVariable123" {}    # camelCase allowed
variable "_private" {}         # can start with underscore

# Invalid identifiers:
# variable "123start" {}       # Cannot start with number
# variable "my-variable" {}    # Hyphens not allowed
# variable "my variable" {}    # Spaces not allowed

# Convention: use snake_case
resource "aws_instance" "web_server" {
  instance_type = var.instance_type
}

# Reference format: <type>.<name>.<attribute>
output "instance_id" {
  value = aws_instance.web_server.id
}
Naming Conventions:
  • snake_case: Recommended for all identifiers
  • Descriptive: Names should be self-documenting
  • Consistent: Follow the same pattern throughout
💬

3.1.4 Comments

💬 Comment Styles
HCL Comment Syntax 
# Single-line comment (hash style - preferred)

// Single-line comment (C-style)

/*
  Multi-line comment
  Can span multiple lines
  Useful for longer explanations
*/

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  # This is the AMI for Ubuntu 22.04
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  
  instance_type = "t2.micro"  // inline comment
  
  /*
   * Block comment for complex explanations
   * This instance hosts our web application
   * and connects to the RDS database
   */
  tags = {
    Name = "WebServer"
  }
}
🔄

3.1.5 HCL vs JSON

📄 Native JSON Support

Terraform accepts both .tf (HCL) and .tf.json (JSON) files. They can be mixed in the same directory.

✅ HCL Format
HCL example.tf 
variable "region" {
  type    = string
  default = "us-east-1"
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-abc123"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  
  tags = {
    Name = "WebServer"
  }
}
📄 JSON Format
JSON example.tf.json 
{
  "variable": {
    "region": {
      "type": "string",
      "default": "us-east-1"
    }
  },
  "resource": {
    "aws_instance": {
      "web": {
        "ami": "ami-abc123",
        "instance_type": "t2.micro",
        "tags": {
          "Name": "WebServer"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
💡
TIP: Use HCL for human-written configuration and JSON for machine-generated configuration (templates, automation tools).

3.2 Variables and Types

CORE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning how to make your Terraform configurations parameterized and reusable. Variables are the input mechanism for Terraform—they let you create a single configuration that can deploy to dev, staging, and production with different settings. This section covers variable declaration, all the type constraints (primitive and complex), validation rules, and the various ways to provide values. Understanding the type system is especially important—it helps Terraform catch errors before you accidentally deploy the wrong thing.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know how to declare variables with description, type, and default values
  • Understand primitive types (string, number, bool) and when to use each
  • Master complex types (list, set, map, object, tuple) for structured data
  • Be able to write custom validation rules for input variables
  • Understand variable precedence and all the ways to set variable values
  • Know how to mark variables as sensitive to protect secrets
📊

📥 Input Variables

Input variables serve as parameters for your Terraform configuration, making it customizable and reusable across environments.

  • Parameterize configurations
  • Type constraints for validation
  • Default values for optional inputs
  • Sensitive flag for secrets
PRIMITIVE
🔤

String

Unicode text values like names, regions, and identifiers.

PRIMITIVE
🔢

Number

Integer and floating-point values for counts and thresholds.

PRIMITIVE
✅

Bool

True/false values for feature toggles and flags.

COLLECTION
📋

List/Tuple

Ordered sequences of values accessed by index.

COLLECTION
🗺️

Map

Key-value pairs for lookups and environment configs.

STRUCTURAL
📦

Object

Named attributes with different types for structured data.

📊 Variable Precedence (Highest to Lowest) 
 ╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
 ║                      VARIABLE VALUE PRECEDENCE                             ║
 ╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
 ║                                                                           ║
 ║   🔝 HIGHEST PRIORITY                                                     ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  -var and -var-file on command line                               │   ║
 ║   │  terraform apply -var="instance_type=t3.large"                    │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  *.auto.tfvars and *.auto.tfvars.json                             │   ║
 ║   │  (automatically loaded, alphabetical order)                       │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  terraform.tfvars or terraform.tfvars.json                        │   ║
 ║   │  (automatically loaded if present)                                │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  Environment variables (TF_VAR_name)                              │   ║
 ║   │  export TF_VAR_instance_type="t2.small"                           │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║                              ▼                                            ║
 ║   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   ║
 ║   │  Default values in variable declaration                           │   ║
 ║   │  variable "region" { default = "us-east-1" }                      │   ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   ║
 ║   🔻 LOWEST PRIORITY                                                      ║
 ║                                                                           ║
 ╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
📝

3.2.1 Declaring Variables

📝 Variable Block Syntax
HCL variables.tf 
# Basic variable declaration
variable "region" {
  description = "AWS region for resources"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

# Required variable (no default)
variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment (dev/staging/prod)"
  type        = string
}

# Variable with validation
variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
  default     = "t2.micro"
  
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^t[23]\\.", var.instance_type))
    error_message = "Instance type must be t2.* or t3.* family."
  }
}

# Sensitive variable (won't show in output)
variable "db_password" {
  description = "Database password"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true
}

# Nullable variable
variable "optional_name" {
  description = "Optional resource name"
  type        = string
  default     = null
  nullable    = true
}
📋 Variable Block Arguments
Argument Required Purpose
description No Documents the variable's purpose
type No* Type constraint (string, number, bool, etc.)
default No Default value if not provided
validation No Custom validation rules
sensitive No Hide value in CLI output
nullable No Allow null value (default: true)
🔤

3.2.2 Primitive Types

🔤 String, Number, Bool
HCL Primitive Type Examples 
# String type
variable "name" {
  type    = string
  default = "my-resource"
}

# Number type (integers and floats)
variable "instance_count" {
  type    = number
  default = 3
}

variable "cpu_threshold" {
  type    = number
  default = 75.5
}

# Boolean type
variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type    = bool
  default = true
}

# Using primitives
resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  count         = var.instance_count           # number
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  monitoring    = var.enable_monitoring        # bool
  
  tags = {
    Name = var.name                            # string
  }
}
Type Example Values Notes
string "hello", "us-east-1" Unicode text in quotes
number 42, 3.14, -10 Integers or floats
bool true, false Boolean values (no quotes)
📦

3.2.3 Complex Types

📦 Collection and Structural Types

Complex types allow grouping multiple values together as lists, maps, or structured objects.

📋 List and Set
HCL List and Set Types 
# List - ordered collection (duplicates allowed)
variable "availability_zones" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
}

# Set - unordered collection (no duplicates)
variable "allowed_ports" {
  type    = set(number)
  default = [80, 443, 22]
}

# Using lists
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = length(var.availability_zones)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]  # Access by index
  cidr_block        = "10.0.${count.index}.0/24"
}

# List of objects
variable "subnets" {
  type = list(object({
    name = string
    cidr = string
    az   = string
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "public-1", cidr = "10.0.1.0/24", az = "us-east-1a" },
    { name = "public-2", cidr = "10.0.2.0/24", az = "us-east-1b" }
  ]
}
🗺️ Map and Object
HCL Map and Object Types 
# Map - collection of key-value pairs (same value type)
variable "instance_types" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    dev     = "t2.micro"
    staging = "t2.small"
    prod    = "t2.large"
  }
}

# Using map
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  instance_type = var.instance_types[var.environment]  # Lookup by key
}

# Object - structured type with named attributes
variable "vpc_config" {
  type = object({
    cidr_block           = string
    enable_dns_hostnames = bool
    enable_dns_support   = bool
    tags                 = map(string)
  })
  default = {
    cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
    enable_dns_hostnames = true
    enable_dns_support   = true
    tags = {
      Environment = "Production"
      Project     = "MyApp"
    }
  }
}

# Using object
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_config.cidr_block
  enable_dns_hostnames = var.vpc_config.enable_dns_hostnames
  enable_dns_support   = var.vpc_config.enable_dns_support
  tags                 = var.vpc_config.tags
}
📊 Tuple Type
HCL Tuple Type 
# Tuple - fixed-length sequence with specific types for each element
variable "server_spec" {
  type = tuple([string, number, bool])
  # Elements: [name, port, enabled]
  default = ["webserver", 8080, true]
}

# Using tuple
locals {
  server_name    = var.server_spec[0]  # "webserver"
  server_port    = var.server_spec[1]  # 8080
  server_enabled = var.server_spec[2]  # true
}
🌐

3.2.4 The any Type

🌐 Flexible Typing with any
HCL any Type Examples 
# 'any' accepts any type - determined at runtime
variable "flexible_tags" {
  type    = map(any)  # Map values can be any type
  default = {
    Name        = "WebServer"
    Port        = 8080           # number is OK
    Enabled     = true           # bool is OK
  }
}

# List of any
variable "mixed_list" {
  type    = list(any)
  default = ["string", 123, true]  # Mixed types
}

# Object with any attribute
variable "config" {
  type = object({
    name     = string
    settings = any  # Can be any structure
  })
}
⚠️
CAUTION: Use any sparingly. Specific types provide better documentation and catch errors earlier. Prefer explicit types when possible.
📊

3.2.5 Variable Precedence

⬆️ Order of Precedence (Lowest to Highest)

When the same variable is set in multiple places, Terraform uses the value with highest precedence.

📊 Precedence Order
📊 Variable Precedence (Lowest → Highest)
Priority (wins)
    ↑
    │  7. -var flag or -var-file on CLI        ← HIGHEST
    │
    │  6. *.auto.tfvars.json
    │
    │  5. *.auto.tfvars (alphabetical order)
    │
    │  4. terraform.tfvars.json
    │
    │  3. terraform.tfvars
    │
    │  2. TF_VAR_name environment variables
    │
    │  1. Default value in variable block       ← LOWEST
    ↓
Bash Precedence Examples 
# 1. Default in variables.tf: region = "us-east-1"

# 2. Environment variable overrides default
export TF_VAR_region="us-west-1"

# 3. terraform.tfvars overrides env var
# Inside terraform.tfvars: region = "us-west-2"

# 4. *.auto.tfvars overrides terraform.tfvars
# Inside dev.auto.tfvars: region = "eu-west-1"

# 5. CLI -var flag overrides everything
terraform apply -var="region=ap-south-1"
📝
EXAM TIP: Variable precedence is a common exam topic. Remember: CLI flags (-var, -var-file) have the highest precedence and override all other sources.
✅

3.2.6 Variable Validation

✅ Custom Validation Rules
HCL Validation Block Examples 
# Validate with list of allowed values
variable "environment" {
  type = string
  
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

# Validate with regex pattern
variable "bucket_name" {
  type = string
  
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9.-]{1,61}[a-z0-9]$", var.bucket_name))
    error_message = "Bucket name must be valid S3 bucket name format."
  }
}

# Validate number range
variable "instance_count" {
  type = number
  
  validation {
    condition     = var.instance_count >= 1 && var.instance_count <= 10
    error_message = "Instance count must be between 1 and 10."
  }
}

# Multiple validations
variable "cidr_block" {
  type = string
  
  validation {
    condition     = can(cidrhost(var.cidr_block, 0))
    error_message = "Must be a valid CIDR notation."
  }
  
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^10\\.", var.cidr_block)) || can(regex("^172\\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[01])\\.", var.cidr_block)) || can(regex("^192\\.168\\.", var.cidr_block))
    error_message = "CIDR must be in private IP range (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x)."
  }
}

3.3 Outputs

CORE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning how to export information from your Terraform configurations. Outputs are the opposite of variables—while variables bring values IN, outputs send values OUT. They're essential for displaying useful information after an apply (like "here's your server's IP"), passing data between modules in a large configuration, and integrating Terraform with scripts and other automation tools. You'll use outputs constantly when working with modules.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know how to declare outputs with value, description, and sensitive flags
  • Understand when and why to use outputs vs. just reading state directly
  • Be able to query outputs using the terraform output command
  • Know how to format output for scripts (-raw, -json flags)
  • Understand how modules expose outputs to their callers
