  • Be able to create complex outputs with computed values
📤

📤 Output Values

Outputs export values from your Terraform configuration. They're useful for displaying information, passing data between modules, and integration with other tools.

  • Display resource attributes after apply
  • Pass data between root and child modules
  • Provide values for scripts and automation
  • Query with terraform output command
📊 Output Value Flow 
 ╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
 ║                          OUTPUT VALUE FLOW                                 ║
 ╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
 ║                                                                           ║
 ║   ┌─────────────────┐                                                     ║
 ║   │  📦 Resources   │                                                     ║
 ║   │  Data Sources   │                                                     ║
 ║   │  Variables      │                                                     ║
 ║   └────────┬────────┘                                                     ║
 ║            │                                                              ║
 ║            ▼                                                              ║
 ║   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     ║
 ║   │                    📤 OUTPUT BLOCKS                              │     ║
 ║   │  output "instance_ip" { value = aws_instance.web.public_ip }    │     ║
 ║   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     ║
 ║            │                                                              ║
 ║            ├───────────────────┬───────────────────┬─────────────────┐    ║
 ║            ▼                   ▼                   ▼                 ▼    ║
 ║   ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐   ┌──────────┐  ║
 ║   │  🖥️ CLI     │     │  👤 Parent  │     │  📜 Scripts │   │ 💾 State │  ║
 ║   │  Display    │     │    Module   │     │  & Tools    │   │   File   │  ║
 ║   │             │     │             │     │             │   │          │  ║
 ║   │ terraform   │     │  module.    │     │ terraform   │   │ Stored   │  ║
 ║   │ output      │     │  vpc.vpc_id │     │ output -json│   │ for      │  ║
 ║   │ instance_ip │     │             │     │ | jq        │   │ reference│  ║
 ║   └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘   └──────────┘  ║
 ║                                                                           ║
 ╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
CLI
🖥️

terraform output

Display values after apply. Use -json for machine parsing, -raw for scripts.

MODULE
🔗

Module Interface

Export values to parent modules: module.vpc.vpc_id for cross-module references.

AUTOMATION
⚙️

CI/CD Integration

Feed values into deployment scripts, configuration management, or other tools.

STATE
💾

Remote State

Access cross-workspace via terraform_remote_state data source.

📝

3.3.1 Output Declaration

📝 Output Block Syntax
HCL outputs.tf 
# Basic output
output "instance_id" {
  description = "The ID of the EC2 instance"
  value       = aws_instance.web.id
}

# Output with sensitive value
output "db_password" {
  description = "The database password"
  value       = aws_db_instance.main.password
  sensitive   = true
}

# Conditional output
output "public_ip" {
  description = "Public IP (if assigned)"
  value       = aws_instance.web.public_ip
  
  # Only set after resource exists
  depends_on = [aws_instance.web]
}

# Complex output value
output "instance_info" {
  description = "Instance details"
  value = {
    id         = aws_instance.web.id
    public_ip  = aws_instance.web.public_ip
    private_ip = aws_instance.web.private_ip
    az         = aws_instance.web.availability_zone
  }
}
⚙️

3.3.2 Output Attributes

📋 Output Block Arguments
Argument Required Purpose
value Yes The value to export (any expression)
description No Documents the output's purpose
sensitive No Hide value in CLI output
depends_on No Explicit dependency on resources
precondition No Validation before exposing value
🔍

3.3.3 Querying Outputs

🔍 terraform output Command
Bash Output Commands 
# List all outputs
terraform output

# Get specific output
terraform output instance_id

# Get output as JSON (for scripts)
terraform output -json

# Get raw value (no quotes for strings)
terraform output -raw instance_id

# Use in scripts
INSTANCE_ID=$(terraform output -raw instance_id)
echo "Instance ID: $INSTANCE_ID"

# Get JSON output for parsing
terraform output -json instance_info | jq '.public_ip'
💻 Example Output Display 
$ terraform output
instance_id = "i-0abc123def456789"
instance_info = {
  "az" = "us-east-1a"
  "id" = "i-0abc123def456789"
  "private_ip" = "10.0.1.50"
  "public_ip" = "54.123.45.67"
}
public_ip = "54.123.45.67"

$ terraform output -raw public_ip
54.123.45.67
📦

3.3.4 Module Outputs

📦 Accessing Module Outputs
HCL Module Output Reference 
# In modules/vpc/outputs.tf
output "vpc_id" {
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "subnet_ids" {
  value = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}

# In root module main.tf
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"
  
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

# Reference module outputs
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  subnet_id = module.vpc.subnet_ids[0]  # First subnet
  
  tags = {
    VPC = module.vpc.vpc_id
  }
}

# Export module outputs from root
output "vpc_id" {
  value = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

3.4 Local Values

CORE
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning to use computed, internal values that simplify your configurations. Locals are like variables, but they're calculated INSIDE your Terraform code rather than passed in from outside. They're perfect for avoiding repetition (like defining common tags once), naming complex expressions to make code readable, and computing derived values. Unlike variables, locals aren't exposed to users—they're purely an internal organizational tool.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know how to declare local values in a locals block
  • Understand the difference between locals and variables (when to use each)
  • Be able to use locals for common tags, naming prefixes, and conditional logic
  • Know how to reference locals with local.name syntax
  • Understand patterns for transforming input data with locals
  • Recognize when to use locals vs. variables vs. outputs
🏠

🏠 Locals - Internal Variables

Local values are named values computed within your configuration. They help reduce repetition and make configurations more readable by giving names to complex expressions.

  • Reduce repetition (DRY principle)
  • Name complex expressions for clarity
  • Compute derived values from inputs
  • Not exposed outside the module
📊 Variables vs Locals vs Outputs 
 ╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
 ║                  DATA FLOW: Variables → Locals → Outputs                   ║
 ╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
 ║                                                                           ║
 ║   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     ║
 ║   │                        📥 VARIABLES                              │     ║
 ║   │  • External input (from user/file/env)                          │     ║
 ║   │  • var.name syntax                                              │     ║
 ║   │  • Exposed to module users                                      │     ║
 ║   │  • Example: var.environment = "prod"                            │     ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘     ║
 ║                               ▼                                           ║
 ║   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     ║
 ║   │                        🏠 LOCALS                                 │     ║
 ║   │  • Internal computed values                                     │     ║
 ║   │  • local.name syntax                                            │     ║
 ║   │  • NOT exposed outside module                                   │     ║
 ║   │  • Example: local.is_prod = var.environment == "prod"           │     ║
 ║   └───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘     ║
 ║                               ▼                                           ║
 ║   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     ║
 ║   │                        📤 OUTPUTS                                │     ║
 ║   │  • Export values out of module                                  │     ║
 ║   │  • output "name" block syntax                                   │     ║
 ║   │  • Accessible via terraform output or module.x.y                │     ║
 ║   │  • Example: output "vpc_id" { value = aws_vpc.main.id }         │     ║
 ║   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     ║
 ║                                                                           ║
 ╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
DRY
🏷️

Common Tags

Define tags once, apply to all resources with merge().

NAMING
📝

Name Prefixes

Build consistent naming conventions: "${local.env}-${local.app}"

COMPUTE
🔄

Transform Data

Convert lists to maps, flatten structures, filter data.

LOGIC
⚡

Conditionals

Calculate environment-specific values: is_prod ? "large" : "small"

📝

3.4.1 Declaring Locals

📝 Locals Block Syntax
HCL locals.tf 
# Locals block - declare multiple local values
locals {
  # Simple values
  project_name = "myapp"
  environment  = "production"
  
  # Computed values
  name_prefix = "${local.project_name}-${local.environment}"
  
  # Common tags (reusable)
  common_tags = {
    Project     = local.project_name
    Environment = local.environment
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    Owner       = "DevOps Team"
  }
  
  # Conditional value
  instance_type = var.environment == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro"
  
  # Timestamp
  timestamp = formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD", timestamp())
}

# Using locals
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  instance_type = local.instance_type
  
  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${local.name_prefix}-web"
  })
}
💡

3.4.2 Using Locals

💡 Common Patterns
HCL Locals Use Cases 
# Pattern 1: Consolidate common tags
locals {
  common_tags = {
    Project     = "WebApp"
    Environment = var.environment
    Terraform   = "true"
  }
}

resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  tags       = local.common_tags
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.main.id
  tags       = merge(local.common_tags, { Name = "public-subnet" })
}

# Pattern 2: Transform input data
locals {
  # Convert list to map for for_each
  subnet_map = { for idx, cidr in var.subnet_cidrs : "subnet-${idx}" => cidr }
  
  # Flatten nested structure
  all_ports = flatten([
    for sg in var.security_groups : [
      for rule in sg.rules : {
        sg_name = sg.name
        port    = rule.port
        cidr    = rule.cidr
      }
    ]
  ])
}

# Pattern 3: Complex conditional logic
locals {
  is_production = var.environment == "prod"
  instance_type = local.is_production ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro"
  multi_az      = local.is_production ? true : false
  backup_retention = local.is_production ? 30 : 7
}
⚖️

3.4.3 Locals vs Variables

📥 Variables (Input)
  • Set externally (tfvars, CLI, env)
  • User-facing parameters
  • Each needs own block
  • Reference: var.name
  • Can have validation rules
🏠 Locals (Internal)
  • Computed within configuration
  • Internal implementation detail
  • Multiple in one block
  • Reference: local.name
  • Cannot be overridden
💡
GUIDELINE: Use variables for values that should be configurable by users. Use locals for values computed from other values that shouldn't change based on user input.

3.5 Data Sources

CORE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning how to read information from existing infrastructure that Terraform doesn't manage. Data sources let you query AWS (or other providers) for existing resources—like finding the latest Amazon Linux AMI, looking up a VPC that was created manually, or getting the list of available availability zones. Unlike resources which CREATE things, data sources only READ. They're essential for integrating with resources created outside Terraform and for dynamic configurations.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know how to declare and use data sources with the data block
  • Understand the difference between data sources (read) and resources (create)
  • Be able to use common AWS data sources (aws_ami, aws_vpc, aws_availability_zones)
  • Know how to reference data source attributes with data.type.name.attribute syntax
  • Understand when to use data sources vs. hard-coding values
  • Be able to query existing infrastructure for use in your configurations
🔍

🔍 Query Existing Resources

Data sources allow Terraform to fetch and compute data from external sources or existing resources not managed by Terraform. They're read-only - they don't create or modify anything.

  • Query existing infrastructure
  • Fetch AMI IDs, AZ lists, etc.
  • Read external files
  • Reference resources from other state
AWS
🖼️

aws_ami

Find latest AMI IDs by name pattern, owner, and filters.

AWS
🌐

aws_vpc

Look up existing VPCs by ID, tags, or CIDR block.

AWS
📍

aws_availability_zones

Get list of available AZs in the current region.

AWS
👤

aws_caller_identity

Get current account ID, user ID, and ARN.

LOCAL
📁

local_file

Read content from local files for templates.

STATE
🔗

terraform_remote_state

Access outputs from another Terraform state file.

📝

3.5.1 Data Block Syntax

📝 Data Source Declaration
HCL data.tf 
# Data source syntax: data "type" "name" {}
# Reference: data.<type>.<name>.<attribute>

# Get the latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]
  
  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"]
  }
  
  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

# Use the data source
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id   # Reference data source
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
}
📐 Data Source vs Resource
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DATA SOURCE                    │   RESOURCE                  │
│  ──────────                     │   ────────                  │
│  data "aws_ami" "latest" {      │   resource "aws_instance" { │
│    ...                          │     ...                     │
│  }                              │   }                         │
│                                 │                             │
│  ✅ READ existing resources     │   ✅ CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE   │
│  ✅ Query external data         │   ✅ Manage infrastructure  │
│  ❌ Cannot modify anything      │   ✅ Tracked in state       │
│                                 │                             │
│  Reference: data.type.name.attr │   Reference: type.name.attr │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📋

3.5.2 Common Data Sources

☁️ AWS Data Sources
HCL Common AWS Data Sources 
# Get current AWS account info
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

output "account_id" {
  value = data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id
}

# Get current region
data "aws_region" "current" {}

# Get list of availability zones
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = length(data.aws_availability_zones.available.names)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  cidr_block        = "10.0.${count.index}.0/24"
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
}

# Get existing VPC by tag
data "aws_vpc" "existing" {
  tags = {
    Name = "production-vpc"
  }
}

# Get existing security group
data "aws_security_group" "existing" {
  vpc_id = data.aws_vpc.existing.id
  
  filter {
    name   = "group-name"
    values = ["web-sg"]
  }
}

# Get IAM policy document
data "aws_iam_policy_document" "s3_access" {
  statement {
    actions   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
    resources = ["arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket", "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"]
  }
}

resource "aws_iam_policy" "s3_access" {
  name   = "s3-read-access"
  policy = data.aws_iam_policy_document.s3_access.json
}
📁

3.5.3 Local Data Sources

📁 File and Template Data
HCL Local Data Sources 
# Read a local file
data "local_file" "ssh_key" {
  filename = "${path.module}/keys/id_rsa.pub"
}

resource "aws_key_pair" "deployer" {
  key_name   = "deployer-key"
  public_key = data.local_file.ssh_key.content
}

# Template file (render variables)
data "template_file" "user_data" {
  template = file("${path.module}/scripts/user_data.tpl")
  
  vars = {
    environment = var.environment
    db_host     = aws_db_instance.main.endpoint
    app_version = var.app_version
  }
}

# Better: use templatefile function instead
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/scripts/user_data.tpl", {
    environment = var.environment
    db_host     = aws_db_instance.main.endpoint
  })
}

# External data source (run script)
data "external" "example" {
  program = ["python3", "${path.module}/scripts/get_info.py"]
  
  query = {
    input_param = "value"
  }
}

# Returns JSON, access as: data.external.example.result.output_key
🔗

3.5.4 Remote State Data Source

🔗 Cross-Project References

Access outputs from another Terraform state file. Essential for splitting infrastructure across projects.

🔗 terraform_remote_state
HCL Remote State Data Source 
# Reference outputs from another state file
data "terraform_remote_state" "network" {
  backend = "s3"
  
  config = {
    bucket = "my-terraform-state"
    key    = "network/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Use outputs from the network state
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  subnet_id         = data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.subnet_id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [
    data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.web_sg_id
  ]
}

# Example: Network project outputs
# In network project's outputs.tf:
output "vpc_id" {
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "subnet_id" {
  value = aws_subnet.public.id
}

output "web_sg_id" {
  value = aws_security_group.web.id
}
📝
EXAM TIP: Data sources are READ-ONLY. They query existing infrastructure but never create, update, or delete resources. The terraform_remote_state data source is commonly tested.

3.6 Expressions and Operators

CORE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning the computational and logical building blocks of HCL. Expressions are how you reference other resources, compute values, make decisions, and transform data. This includes arithmetic (+, -, *, /), comparisons (==, !=, <, >), conditional logic (if-then-else with ?:), and powerful for-expressions that transform collections. These tools let you write dynamic, flexible infrastructure code that adapts to different environments and inputs.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know all reference types (var., local., data., module., path., terraform.)
  • Understand arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators
  • Master conditional expressions (ternary operator ? :)
  • Be able to use for expressions to transform lists and maps
  • Understand splat expressions ([*]) for extracting attributes from collections
  • Know string interpolation and template syntax
🔢

🔢 Expressions in HCL

Expressions are used to compute values. They can reference resources, call functions, perform arithmetic, and use conditional logic.

  • Arithmetic and comparison operators
  • Conditional expressions (ternary)
  • For expressions for transformation
  • Splat expressions for lists
REFERENCE
🔗

References

var., local., data., module., path., terraform. prefixes for accessing values.

MATH
➕

Arithmetic

+, -, *, /, % operators for numeric calculations and computations.

COMPARE
⚖️

Comparison

==, !=, <, >, <=, >= for equality and ordering tests.

LOGIC
🔀

Logical

&&, ||, ! for combining boolean conditions.

CONDITIONAL
❓

Ternary

condition ? true_val : false_val for if-else decisions.

TRANSFORM
🔄

For Expressions

[for x in list : x.attr] for transforming collections.

📊 Reference Types Quick Reference 
 ╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
 ║                      HCL REFERENCE TYPES                                   ║
 ╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
 ║                                                                           ║
 ║   PREFIX          SYNTAX                    EXAMPLE                       ║
 ║   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   ║
 ║   📦 Resources    TYPE.NAME.ATTR            aws_vpc.main.id               ║
 ║   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   ║
 ║   📥 Variables    var.NAME                  var.environment               ║
 ║   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   ║
 ║   🏠 Locals       local.NAME                local.common_tags             ║
 ║   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   ║
 ║   🔍 Data         data.TYPE.NAME.ATTR       data.aws_ami.latest.id        ║
 ║   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   ║
 ║   📦 Modules      module.NAME.OUTPUT        module.vpc.vpc_id             ║
 ║   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   ║
 ║   📁 Path         path.module               ${path.module}/scripts/       ║
 ║                   path.root                 ${path.root}/configs/         ║
 ║                   path.cwd                  ${path.cwd}                   ║
 ║   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────   ║
 ║   🏷️ Terraform    terraform.workspace       terraform.workspace           ║
 ║                                                                           ║
 ╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
🔗

3.6.1 References

🔗 Reference Syntax
HCL Reference Examples 
# Resource reference: <resource_type>.<name>.<attribute>
vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

# Data source reference: data.<type>.<name>.<attribute>
ami_id = data.aws_ami.latest.id

# Variable reference: var.<name>
region = var.aws_region

# Local reference: local.<name>
tags = local.common_tags

# Module output: module.<name>.<output>
subnet_id = module.vpc.public_subnet_id

# Path references
user_data = file("${path.module}/scripts/init.sh")
# path.module  - directory of current module
# path.root    - root module directory
# path.cwd     - current working directory

# terraform workspace: terraform.workspace
environment = terraform.workspace
➕

3.6.2 Operators

➕ Arithmetic and Logical Operators
HCL Operator Examples 
locals {
  # Arithmetic operators
  sum        = 5 + 3      # 8
  difference = 10 - 4     # 6
  product    = 3 * 4      # 12
  quotient   = 20 / 5     # 4
  remainder  = 17 % 5     # 2
  negative   = -5         # -5
  
  # Comparison operators (return bool)
  equal        = 5 == 5   # true
  not_equal    = 5 != 3   # true
  less         = 3 < 5    # true
  greater      = 5 > 3    # true
  less_eq      = 5 <= 5   # true
  greater_eq   = 5 >= 3   # true
  
  # Logical operators
  and_result = true && false  # false
  or_result  = true || false  # true
  not_result = !true          # false
  
  # Practical examples
  instance_count = var.environment == "prod" ? 3 : 1
  
  is_production  = var.environment == "prod" && var.enable_ha
  
  should_create  = var.create_resource != false
}
Category Operators
Arithmetic +, -, *, /, %, -(negation)
Comparison ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=
Logical &&, ||, !
❓

3.6.3 Conditional Expressions

❓ Ternary Operator
HCL Conditional Expression Syntax 
# Syntax: condition ? true_value : false_value

# Basic conditional
instance_type = var.environment == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro"

# Nested conditionals (avoid if possible)
instance_type = (
  var.environment == "prod" ? "t3.large" :
  var.environment == "staging" ? "t3.medium" :
  "t3.micro"  # default
)

# Conditional resource creation with count
resource "aws_eip" "example" {
  count    = var.create_eip ? 1 : 0
  instance = aws_instance.web.id
}

# Conditional value or null
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  # Assign EIP only if created
  associate_public_ip_address = var.create_eip ? false : true
}

# Conditional with for_each
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "https" {
  for_each = var.enable_https ? toset(["443"]) : toset([])
  
  type        = "ingress"
  from_port   = each.value
  to_port     = each.value
  protocol    = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.web.id
}
🔄

3.6.4 For Expressions

🔄 Transform Collections

For expressions transform one collection into another, similar to list comprehensions in Python.

🔄 For Expression Syntax
HCL For Expression Examples 
# For expression creating a list: [for item in collection : expression]
# For expression creating a map: {for item in collection : key => value}

locals {
  # Transform list to uppercase
  names = ["alice", "bob", "charlie"]
  upper_names = [for name in local.names : upper(name)]
  
  # Transform list to map
  name_map = {for name in local.names : name => upper(name)}
  
  # Filter with if clause
  long_names = [for name in local.names : name if length(name) > 3]
  
  # With index
  indexed = [for i, name in local.names : "${i}: ${name}"]
  
  # Transform map
  instance_types = {
    web = "t3.micro"
    api = "t3.small"
    db  = "t3.medium"
  }
  
  # Map keys
  instance_names = [for k, v in local.instance_types : k]
  
  # Transform map values
  instance_tags = {for k, v in local.instance_types : k => "Instance: ${v}"}
}

# Practical: Create subnet map for for_each
variable "subnets" {
  default = [
    { name = "public-1", cidr = "10.0.1.0/24", az = "a" },
    { name = "public-2", cidr = "10.0.2.0/24", az = "b" }
  ]
}

locals {
  subnet_map = {for subnet in var.subnets : subnet.name => subnet}
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  for_each = local.subnet_map
  
  cidr_block        = each.value.cidr
  availability_zone = "${var.region}${each.value.az}"
  
  tags = { Name = each.key }
}
upper_names = ["ALICE", "BOB", "CHARLIE"] name_map = { "alice" = "ALICE", "bob" = "BOB", "charlie" = "CHARLIE" } long_names = ["alice", "charlie"] indexed = ["0: alice", "1: bob", "2: charlie"] instance_names = ["web", "api", "db"] instance_tags = { "web" = "Instance: t3.micro", "api" = "Instance: t3.small", "db" = "Instance: t3.medium" }
✨

3.6.5 Splat Expressions

✨ Splat Operator [*]
HCL Splat Expression Examples 
# Splat expressions extract attributes from lists

# Create multiple instances
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count         = 3
  ami           = "ami-abc123"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
}

# Get all instance IDs with splat
output "instance_ids" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].id
}

# Equivalent for expression
output "instance_ids_for" {
  value = [for instance in aws_instance.web : instance.id]
}

# Get all private IPs
output "private_ips" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].private_ip
}

# Legacy splat (still works)
output "ids_legacy" {
  value = aws_instance.web.*.id
}

# Splat with nested attributes
variable "users" {
  default = [
    { name = "alice", roles = ["admin", "user"] },
    { name = "bob", roles = ["user"] }
  ]
}

output "all_names" {
  value = var.users[*].name
}
instance_ids = ["i-111", "i-222", "i-333"] all_names = ["alice", "bob"]
💡
TIP: Use [*] (splat) for simple attribute extraction from lists. Use for expressions when you need filtering, transformation, or complex logic.
⚡

3.6.6 Dynamic Blocks

⚡ Generate Nested Blocks Dynamically
HCL Dynamic Block Example 
# Dynamic blocks generate repeated nested blocks

variable "ingress_rules" {
  default = [
    { port = 80,  cidr = "0.0.0.0/0", description = "HTTP" },
    { port = 443, cidr = "0.0.0.0/0", description = "HTTPS" },
    { port = 22,  cidr = "10.0.0.0/8", description = "SSH internal" }
  ]
}

resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name        = "web-sg"
  description = "Web security group"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id
  
  # Dynamic block generates multiple ingress blocks
  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = var.ingress_rules
    
    content {
      from_port   = ingress.value.port
      to_port     = ingress.value.port
      protocol    = "tcp"
      cidr_blocks = [ingress.value.cidr]
      description = ingress.value.description
    }
  }
  
  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
}

# The above is equivalent to writing:
# ingress {
#   from_port = 80
#   ...
# }
# ingress {
#   from_port = 443
#   ...
# }
# ingress {
#   from_port = 22
#   ...
# }
Dynamic Block Components:
  • for_each: Collection to iterate over
  • iterator: Optional custom name (default: block name)
  • content: The nested block to generate
  • ingress.key: Map key or list index
  • ingress.value: The current element

3.7 Functions Overview

CORE 📝 EXAM TOPIC REFERENCE
🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're exploring Terraform's extensive library of built-in functions. Functions transform, combine, and compute values—from simple string manipulation (upper(), replace()) to powerful collection operations (merge(), flatten(), lookup()). Unlike general-purpose programming languages, you can't define custom functions in Terraform, so knowing what's built-in is essential. This section is both a learning guide and a reference you'll return to frequently.

After completing this section, you will:
  • Know the most commonly used string functions (format, join, split, replace, trim)
  • Understand collection functions for lists and maps (length, merge, flatten, lookup)
  • Be able to use type conversion functions (tostring, tolist, tomap, tonumber)
  • Know filesystem functions (file, templatefile, fileexists)
  • Understand encoding functions (jsonencode, base64encode, yamlencode)
  • Be able to use the terraform console to test functions interactively
🔧

🔧 Built-in Functions

Terraform provides many built-in functions for transforming and combining values. Functions are called with function_name(arg1, arg2, ...) syntax.

  • String manipulation
  • Collection operations
  • Type conversions
  • Encoding/decoding
100+ Built-in Functions
8 Function Categories
0 Custom Functions
∞ Combinations
STRING
🔤

String Functions

format(), join(), split(), replace(), trim(), upper(), lower(), substr()

COLLECTION
📋

Collection Functions

length(), merge(), flatten(), lookup(), keys(), values(), concat(), element()

NUMERIC
🔢

Numeric Functions

min(), max(), abs(), ceil(), floor(), log(), pow(), parseint()

TYPE
🔄

Type Functions

tostring(), tonumber(), tobool(), tolist(), toset(), tomap()

ENCODE
🔐

Encoding Functions

jsonencode(), jsondecode(), base64encode(), yamlencode(), urlencode()

FILE
📁

Filesystem Functions

file(), fileexists(), fileset(), templatefile(), pathexpand()

💡
PRO TIP: Use terraform console to interactively test functions before using them in your configuration. Type expressions and see results immediately!
🔤

3.7.1 String Functions

🔤 Common String Functions
HCL String Function Examples 
locals {
  # format - printf-style formatting
  formatted = format("Hello, %s! Count: %d", "World", 42)
  
  # join - join list elements
  joined = join("-", ["a", "b", "c"])
  
  # split - split string to list
  parts = split(",", "a,b,c")
  
  # upper / lower - case conversion
  upper_text = upper("hello")
  lower_text = lower("HELLO")
  title_text = title("hello world")
  
  # replace - string replacement
  replaced = replace("hello-world", "-", "_")
  
  # trim functions
  trimmed       = trim("  hello  ", " ")
  trim_prefix   = trimprefix("helloworld", "hello")
  trim_suffix   = trimsuffix("helloworld", "world")
  
  # substr - substring
  sub = substr("hello", 0, 3)
  
  # regex / regexall
  match = regex("[a-z]+", "123abc456")
  matches = regexall("[0-9]+", "a1b2c3")
}
formatted = "Hello, World! Count: 42" joined = "a-b-c" parts = ["a", "b", "c"] upper_text = "HELLO" lower_text = "hello" title_text = "Hello World" replaced = "hello_world" trimmed = "hello" trim_prefix = "world" trim_suffix = "hello" sub = "hel" match = "abc" matches = ["1", "2", "3"]
📋

3.7.2 Collection Functions

📋 List, Map, and Set Functions
HCL Collection Function Examples 
locals {
  # length - count elements
  list_len = length(["a", "b", "c"])  # 3
  map_len  = length({ a = 1, b = 2 })  # 2
  
  # element - get list element by index
  elem = element(["a", "b", "c"], 1)  # "b"
  
  # lookup - get map value with default
  value = lookup({ a = 1, b = 2 }, "a", 0)  # 1
  default_val = lookup({ a = 1 }, "c", 0)  # 0 (default)
  
  # concat - merge lists
  merged_list = concat(["a"], ["b", "c"])  # ["a", "b", "c"]
  
  # merge - merge maps (later wins)
  merged_map = merge({ a = 1 }, { b = 2, a = 3 })
  
  # flatten - flatten nested lists
  flat = flatten([["a", "b"], ["c"]])  # ["a", "b", "c"]
  
  # keys / values - extract from map
  map_keys   = keys({ a = 1, b = 2 })    # ["a", "b"]
  map_values = values({ a = 1, b = 2 })  # [1, 2]
  
  # contains - check if list contains value
  has_item = contains(["a", "b", "c"], "b")  # true
  
  # distinct - remove duplicates
  unique = distinct(["a", "b", "a", "c"])  # ["a", "b", "c"]
  
  # sort - sort list
  sorted = sort(["c", "a", "b"])  # ["a", "b", "c"]
  
  # reverse - reverse list
  reversed = reverse(["a", "b", "c"])  # ["c", "b", "a"]
  
  # slice - get sublist
  sliced = slice(["a", "b", "c", "d"], 1, 3)  # ["b", "c"]
  
  # zipmap - create map from two lists
  zipped = zipmap(["a", "b"], [1, 2])  # { a = 1, b = 2 }
}
merged_map = { a = 3, b = 2 }
🔢

3.7.3 Numeric Functions

🔢 Math and Number Functions
HCL Numeric Function Examples 
locals {
  # min / max
  minimum = min(5, 12, 9)     # 5
  maximum = max(5, 12, 9)     # 12
  
  # abs - absolute value
  absolute = abs(-23)         # 23
  
  # ceil / floor - rounding
  rounded_up   = ceil(4.1)    # 5
  rounded_down = floor(4.9)   # 4
  
  # pow - power
  power = pow(2, 8)           # 256
  
  # log - logarithm
  logarithm = log(100, 10)    # 2
  
  # signum - sign (-1, 0, 1)
  sign_neg  = signum(-5)      # -1
  sign_zero = signum(0)       # 0
  sign_pos  = signum(5)       # 1
  
  # parseint - string to int
  parsed = parseint("FF", 16)  # 255
}
🔄

3.7.4 Type Conversion Functions

🔄 Type Conversion
HCL Type Conversion Examples 
locals {
  # tostring / tonumber / tobool
  str_val  = tostring(123)     # "123"
  num_val  = tonumber("123")   # 123
  bool_val = tobool("true")    # true
  
  # tolist / toset / tomap
  as_list = tolist(["a", "b"])
  as_set  = toset(["a", "b", "a"])  # removes duplicates
  as_map  = tomap({ a = 1, b = 2 })
  
  # type() - get type (debugging)
  type_info = type(["a", "b"])  # tuple
  
  # can() - test if expression is valid
  valid = can(regex("^[a-z]+$", var.bucket_name))
  
  # try() - return first valid expression
  result = try(var.optional_value, "default")
  
  # coalesce - first non-null/empty value
  first_value = coalesce("", null, "hello")  # "hello"
  
  # coalescelist - first non-empty list
  first_list = coalescelist([], ["a", "b"])  # ["a", "b"]
}

# Practical use of can() and try()
variable "json_config" {
  type    = string
  default = ""
}

locals {
  # Safely decode JSON or use default
  config = can(jsondecode(var.json_config)) ? jsondecode(var.json_config) : {}
  
  # Or with try()
  config_v2 = try(jsondecode(var.json_config), {})
}
📁

3.7.5 Filesystem Functions

📁 File Operations
HCL Filesystem Functions 
locals {
  # file - read file contents
  script_content = file("${path.module}/scripts/init.sh")
  
  # fileexists - check if file exists
  has_config = fileexists("${path.module}/config.json")
  
  # filebase64 - read file as base64
  encoded = filebase64("${path.module}/files/image.png")
  
  # templatefile - render template with variables
  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/templates/user_data.tpl", {
    hostname    = "webserver"
    environment = var.environment
  })
  
  # fileset - list files matching pattern
  config_files = fileset("${path.module}/configs", "*.json")
  
  # dirname / basename - path operations
  dir  = dirname("/path/to/file.txt")   # "/path/to"
  base = basename("/path/to/file.txt")  # "file.txt"
  
  # abspath - absolute path
  abs_path = abspath(path.module)
  
  # pathexpand - expand ~ to home directory
  home_path = pathexpand("~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")
}

# Common use: Lambda function code
resource "aws_lambda_function" "example" {
  filename      = "${path.module}/lambda.zip"
  function_name = "example"
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "python3.9"
  
  source_code_hash = filebase64sha256("${path.module}/lambda.zip")
}
🔐

3.7.6 Encoding Functions

🔐 Encoding and Hashing
HCL Encoding Function Examples 
locals {
  # JSON encoding/decoding
  json_string = jsonencode({ name = "test", count = 1 })
  
  json_object = jsondecode("{\"name\":\"test\"}")
  
  # YAML encoding/decoding
  yaml_string = yamlencode({ name = "test", items = ["a", "b"] })
  yaml_object = yamldecode("name: test\ncount: 1")
  
  # Base64 encoding/decoding
  encoded_b64 = base64encode("Hello, World!")
  decoded_b64 = base64decode("SGVsbG8sIFdvcmxkIQ==")
  
  # URL encoding
  url_encoded = urlencode("hello world")  # "hello%20world"
  
  # Hash functions
  md5_hash    = md5("hello")
  sha1_hash   = sha1("hello")
  sha256_hash = sha256("hello")
  sha512_hash = sha512("hello")
  
  # File hash
  file_sha256 = filesha256("${path.module}/script.sh")
  file_b64sha = filebase64sha256("${path.module}/lambda.zip")
  
  # UUID generation
  random_id = uuid()  # "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-..."
}

# Practical: IAM policy as JSON
resource "aws_iam_policy" "example" {
  name = "example-policy"
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect   = "Allow"
        Action   = ["s3:GetObject"]
        Resource = ["arn:aws:s3:::bucket/*"]
      }
    ]
  })
}
json_string = "{\"name\":\"test\",\"count\":1}" json_object = { name = "test" }
📅

3.7.7 Date and Time Functions

📅 Date/Time Functions
HCL Date/Time Examples 
locals {
  # timestamp - current UTC time (RFC 3339)
  now = timestamp()
  
  # formatdate - format timestamp
  date_only = formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD", timestamp())
  
  readable = formatdate("DD MMM YYYY hh:mm:ss", timestamp())
  
  # timeadd - add duration to timestamp
  future = timeadd(timestamp(), "720h")  # 30 days later
  
  # timecmp - compare timestamps (-1, 0, 1)
  comparison = timecmp("2024-01-01T00:00:00Z", "2024-01-02T00:00:00Z")
  
  # Practical: set expiration date
  cert_valid_until = timeadd(timestamp(), "8760h")  # 1 year
}

resource "aws_instance" "example" {
  tags = {
    CreatedAt = timestamp()
    ExpiresAt = local.cert_valid_until
  }
  
  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [tags["CreatedAt"]]  # Don't update on every apply
  }
}
now = "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z" date_only = "2024-01-15" readable = "15 Jan 2024 10:30:00" comparison = -1 (first timestamp is earlier)
📝
EXAM TIP: Know the most common functions: file(), templatefile(), lookup(), merge(), join(), split(), format(), length(), contains(), and jsonencode().

📌 Chapter 3 Summary

Key Takeaways
  • HCL Basics: Blocks, arguments, identifiers, comments
  • Variables: Input parameters with type constraints and validation
  • Types: Primitives (string, number, bool) and complex (list, map, object)
  • Outputs: Export values for use outside the module
  • Locals: Internal computed values for DRY code
  • Data Sources: Query existing resources (read-only)
  • Expressions: Conditionals, for expressions, splat, dynamic blocks
  • Functions: Built-in library for string, collection, encoding operations
Exam Focus Points
  • ✅ Variable precedence order (CLI > auto.tfvars > tfvars > env > default)
  • ✅ Difference between var.x, local.x, and data.type.name.x
  • ✅ Data sources are READ-ONLY
  • ✅ For expressions: [for x in list : expr] and {for k, v in map : key => value}
  • ✅ Splat expression: resource[*].attribute
  • ✅ Common functions: file, lookup, merge, format, length
← Previous: Chapter 2 - Installation & Environment Next: Chapter 4 - Resources →
📁
📄
⚙️
🗂️

04 Terraform Configuration Files

Master the art of organizing Terraform projects with proper file structure and naming conventions. Learn how each configuration file serves a specific purpose—from main.tf holding your resources, to variables.tf declaring inputs, outputs.tf exposing values, and providers.tf configuring your cloud backends. This chapter transforms you from someone who dumps everything in one file to a professional who creates maintainable, team-friendly, and scalable Terraform codebases.

🎯 Best Practices 📁 Organization ⏱️ 40 Minutes
📚 10 Sections
📄 8+ File Types
🏗️ Pro Patterns
👥 Team Ready

Chapter 4 Terraform Configuration Files

PART 2 CORE CONCEPTS Difficulty: ★★☆☆☆

Professional Terraform projects follow consistent file organization patterns. This chapter covers every standard configuration file—from main.tf where you define resources, to terraform.tf for version constraints, locals.tf for computed values, and the powerful import blocks introduced in Terraform 1.5+. You'll learn not just what goes where, but why this organization matters for team collaboration, code reviews, and long-term maintenance.

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Understand Standard File Structure — Know the purpose of main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, providers.tf, terraform.tf, locals.tf, data.tf, and versions.tf
  • ✅ Organize Resources Effectively — Group related resources logically and understand when to split into multiple files
  • ✅ Configure Provider Settings — Separate provider configuration for multi-region and multi-account deployments
  • ✅ Master Version Constraints — Lock Terraform and provider versions for reproducible deployments
  • ✅ Use Import Blocks (TF 1.5+) — Declaratively import existing resources into Terraform management
  • ✅ Apply Best Practices — Follow conventions that make your code readable, maintainable, and team-friendly

📑 In This Chapter

4.1 main.tf - Primary Configuration 4.2 variables.tf - Input Variables 4.3 outputs.tf - Output Values 4.4 providers.tf - Provider Configuration 4.5 terraform.tf - Settings Block 4.6 locals.tf - Local Values 4.7 data.tf - Data Sources 4.8 versions.tf - Version Constraints 4.9 backend.tf - Backend Config 4.10 Import Blocks (TF 1.5+)
📁

Project Organization

🗂️ Standard Terraform Project Structure 
terraform-project/
│
├── 📄 main.tf              # Primary resources and module calls
├── 📊 variables.tf         # Input variable declarations
├── 📤 outputs.tf           # Output value definitions
├── ☁️  providers.tf         # Provider configurations (AWS, Azure, GCP)
├── ⚙️  terraform.tf         # Terraform settings & required versions
├── 🔧 locals.tf            # Local values (computed variables)
├── 🔍 data.tf              # Data source definitions
├── 🔒 backend.tf           # Remote state backend configuration
│
├── 📋 terraform.tfvars     # Variable values (don't commit secrets!)
├── 📋 prod.tfvars          # Production-specific values
├── 📋 dev.tfvars           # Development-specific values
│
├── 🔐 .terraform.lock.hcl  # Provider dependency lock (commit this!)
├── 📝 .gitignore           # Ignore .terraform/, *.tfstate, etc.
└── 📚 README.md            # Project documentation

modules/                     # Reusable modules directory
├── vpc/
│   ├── main.tf
│   ├── variables.tf
│   └── outputs.tf
│
└── ec2-instance/
    ├── main.tf
    ├── variables.tf
    └── outputs.tf
📄

Configuration Files

Learn the purpose of each .tf file and what content belongs where

🏗️

Best Practices

Follow conventions that scale from solo projects to enterprise teams

🔄

Version Control

Understand what to commit, what to ignore, and how to handle secrets

📥

Import Blocks

Declaratively bring existing infrastructure under Terraform management

4.1 main.tf - Primary Configuration

CORE FILE ESSENTIAL
🎯

What You're Doing Here

main.tf is the heart of your Terraform project. It's where you define the resources that create your actual infrastructure. Think of it as the main entry point—like main() in a programming language. When someone opens your project, main.tf is typically the first file they'll read to understand what you're building.

4.1.1 Resource Definitions

The primary purpose of main.tf is to contain your resource blocks. Each resource block tells Terraform to create and manage a piece of infrastructure:

HCL main.tf - Resource Definitions 
# ============================================================================
# main.tf - Primary Configuration File
# ============================================================================
# This file contains the core infrastructure resources for our project.
# Related: variables.tf (inputs), outputs.tf (exports), providers.tf (auth)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# VPC NETWORKING
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_cidr
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true

  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-vpc"
  })
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count                   = length(var.public_subnet_cidrs)
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block              = var.public_subnet_cidrs[count.index]
  availability_zone       = var.availability_zones[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true

  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-public-${count.index + 1}"
    Tier = "Public"
  })
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# COMPUTE RESOURCES
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count                  = var.instance_count
  ami                    = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type          = var.instance_type
  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.public[count.index % length(aws_subnet.public)].id
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web.id]

  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-web-${count.index + 1}"
    Role = "WebServer"
  })
}
💡

Pro Tip: File Organization in main.tf

Group related resources with comment headers. This makes it easy to navigate large files. Consider splitting into multiple files (network.tf, compute.tf, storage.tf) when main.tf exceeds ~300 lines.

4.1.2 Data Source Definitions

Data sources query existing infrastructure. While you can put them in main.tf, many teams prefer a separate data.tf file:

HCL Data Sources in main.tf 
# Data sources to query existing infrastructure
# (Some teams move these to data.tf)

data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"]
  }
}

data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

data "aws_region" "current" {}

4.1.3 Module Calls

Module blocks call reusable modules—either your own or from the Terraform Registry:

HCL Module Calls in main.tf 
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# MODULE CALLS
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Local module for VPC
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"

  vpc_cidr           = var.vpc_cidr
  environment        = var.environment
  availability_zones = var.availability_zones
}

# Registry module for EKS
module "eks" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws"
  version = "~> 19.0"

  cluster_name    = "${var.project_name}-eks"
  cluster_version = "1.28"

  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids

  eks_managed_node_groups = {
    default = {
      min_size     = 2
      max_size     = 10
      desired_size = 3

      instance_types = ["t3.medium"]
    }
  }

  tags = local.common_tags
}

4.1.4 Organization Strategies

As projects grow, you have several options for organizing main.tf:

Strategy When to Use Example Files
Single main.tf Small projects (<100 lines) main.tf
Split by Resource Type Medium projects (100-500 lines) network.tf, compute.tf, storage.tf, iam.tf
Split by Service Large projects with distinct services api-gateway.tf, lambda.tf, dynamodb.tf
Use Modules Enterprise & multi-environment modules/vpc/, modules/eks/, main.tf (calls only)
SMALL
📄

Flat Structure

All in one main.tf. Good for simple POCs and learning.

MEDIUM
📁

Split Files

Multiple .tf files by resource type. Great for teams.

ENTERPRISE
🏗️

Module-Based

Reusable modules called from main.tf. Maximum flexibility.

4.2 variables.tf - Input Variables

CORE FILE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

variables.tf declares all inputs your configuration accepts. It's like defining function parameters—what values can users provide to customize the deployment? Good variable design makes your Terraform code reusable across environments (dev, staging, prod) without changing the actual resource definitions.

4.2.1 Variable Declarations

HCL variables.tf - Complete Example 
# ============================================================================
# variables.tf - Input Variable Declarations
# ============================================================================
# Define all customizable inputs for this Terraform configuration.
# Values can be set via: CLI (-var), files (.tfvars), env (TF_VAR_*), or defaults.

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# PROJECT METADATA
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
variable "project_name" {
  description = "Name of the project. Used in resource naming and tagging."
  type        = string

  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z0-9-]+$", var.project_name))
    error_message = "Project name must contain only lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens."
  }
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment (dev, staging, prod)"
  type        = string

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be one of: dev, staging, prod."
  }
}

variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region for deployment"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# NETWORKING
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
variable "vpc_cidr" {
  description = "CIDR block for the VPC"
  type        = string
  default     = "10.0.0.0/16"

  validation {
    condition     = can(cidrhost(var.vpc_cidr, 0))
    error_message = "VPC CIDR must be a valid IPv4 CIDR block."
  }
}

variable "public_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "List of CIDR blocks for public subnets"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
}

variable "availability_zones" {
  description = "List of availability zones to use"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# COMPUTE
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
  default     = "t3.micro"
}

variable "instance_count" {
  description = "Number of EC2 instances to create"
  type        = number
  default     = 2

  validation {
    condition     = var.instance_count >= 1 && var.instance_count <= 10
    error_message = "Instance count must be between 1 and 10."
  }
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SENSITIVE DATA
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
variable "db_password" {
  description = "Password for the RDS database"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true  # Won't show in logs or plan output
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# COMPLEX TYPES
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
variable "tags" {
  description = "Additional tags to apply to all resources"
  type        = map(string)
  default     = {}
}

variable "instance_config" {
  description = "Advanced instance configuration"
  type = object({
    instance_type   = string
    volume_size     = number
    volume_type     = string
    enable_monitoring = bool
  })
  default = {
    instance_type     = "t3.micro"
    volume_size       = 20
    volume_type       = "gp3"
    enable_monitoring = true
  }
}

4.2.2 Grouping Related Variables

Organize variables into logical groups using comments. This improves readability:

📊 Variable Organization Pattern 
variables.tf
│
├── 🏷️ PROJECT METADATA
│   ├── project_name      (required)
│   ├── environment       (required)
│   └── owner             (optional)
│
├── 🌐 NETWORKING
│   ├── vpc_cidr          (default: 10.0.0.0/16)
│   ├── subnet_cidrs      (list of CIDRs)
│   └── availability_zones (list of AZs)
│
├── 💻 COMPUTE
│   ├── instance_type     (default: t3.micro)
│   ├── instance_count    (validation: 1-10)
│   └── ami_id            (optional override)
│
├── 💾 DATABASE
│   ├── db_instance_class  (default: db.t3.micro)
│   ├── db_name           (required)
│   └── db_password       (sensitive)
│
└── 🏷️ TAGS
    └── additional_tags    (map, default: {})

4.2.3 Variable Documentation

Well-documented variables make your modules user-friendly:

📝
description

Always include. Shown in terraform plan and generated docs.

🔤
type

Required for type safety. Prevents runtime errors.

🎯
default

Makes variable optional. Omit to require user input.

✅
validation

Catch errors early. Provides custom error messages.

4.3 outputs.tf - Output Values

CORE FILE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

outputs.tf exposes values from your configuration. These values appear after terraform apply, can be queried with terraform output, and are available to other configurations via terraform_remote_state or parent modules. Think of outputs as the "return values" of your Terraform code.

4.3.1 Output Declarations

HCL outputs.tf - Complete Example 
# ============================================================================
# outputs.tf - Output Value Definitions
# ============================================================================
# Expose important values for other configurations, scripts, or users.

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# VPC OUTPUTS
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
output "vpc_id" {
  description = "ID of the created VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "vpc_cidr" {
  description = "CIDR block of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.cidr_block
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  description = "List of public subnet IDs"
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
}

output "public_subnet_cidrs" {
  description = "List of public subnet CIDR blocks"
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].cidr_block
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# COMPUTE OUTPUTS
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
output "instance_ids" {
  description = "List of EC2 instance IDs"
  value       = aws_instance.web[*].id
}

output "instance_public_ips" {
  description = "List of public IP addresses"
  value       = aws_instance.web[*].public_ip
}

output "instance_private_ips" {
  description = "List of private IP addresses"
  value       = aws_instance.web[*].private_ip
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LOAD BALANCER OUTPUT
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
output "alb_dns_name" {
  description = "DNS name of the Application Load Balancer"
  value       = aws_lb.main.dns_name
}

output "alb_zone_id" {
  description = "Zone ID of the ALB (for Route53 alias records)"
  value       = aws_lb.main.zone_id
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# CONNECTION STRINGS (Often sensitive)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
output "db_endpoint" {
  description = "RDS database endpoint"
  value       = aws_db_instance.main.endpoint
}

output "db_connection_string" {
  description = "Database connection string"
  value       = "postgresql://${var.db_username}:${var.db_password}@${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}/${var.db_name}"
  sensitive   = true  # Contains password
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# USEFUL CONSTRUCTED VALUES
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
output "ssh_commands" {
  description = "SSH commands to connect to instances"
  value = [
    for i, instance in aws_instance.web :
    "ssh -i ${var.key_name}.pem ec2-user@${instance.public_ip}"
  ]
}

output "environment_info" {
  description = "Summary of deployed environment"
  value = {
    environment   = var.environment
    region        = var.aws_region
    vpc_id        = aws_vpc.main.id
    instance_count = length(aws_instance.web)
    alb_dns       = aws_lb.main.dns_name
  }
}

4.3.2 Sensitive Outputs

Mark outputs as sensitive to prevent them from showing in logs:

HCL Sensitive Outputs 
# Mark sensitive to hide from terraform plan/apply output
output "db_password" {
  description = "Database password (sensitive)"
  value       = random_password.db.result
  sensitive   = true
}

# To view: terraform output db_password
⚠️

Important: Sensitive Output Behavior

sensitive = true hides the value from terminal output, but it's still stored in plaintext in state files. Always encrypt your state backend!

4.3.3 Module Output Exposure

When building modules, outputs define what the module "returns" to callers:

📤 Module Output Flow 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        ROOT MODULE                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  module "vpc" {                                                  │
│    source = "./modules/vpc"                                      │
│  }                                                               │
│                                                                  │
│  module "eks" {                                                  │
│    vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id        ◄── Uses VPC output    │
│    subnet_ids = module.vpc.subnet_ids    ◄── Uses VPC output    │
│  }                                                               │
│                                                                  │
│  output "cluster_endpoint" {                                     │
│    value = module.eks.endpoint           ◄── Exposes EKS output │
│  }                                                               │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    │                         │
                    ▼                         ▼
┌──────────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────────┐
│    modules/vpc/          │  │    modules/eks/          │
├──────────────────────────┤  ├──────────────────────────┤
│ outputs.tf:              │  │ outputs.tf:              │
│                          │  │                          │
│ output "vpc_id" {        │  │ output "endpoint" {      │
│   value = aws_vpc.id    │  │   value = aws_eks.ep    │
│ }                        │  │ }                        │
│                          │  │                          │
│ output "subnet_ids" {    │  │ output "cluster_name" {  │
│   value = aws_sub[*].id │  │   value = aws_eks.name  │
│ }                        │  │ }                        │
└──────────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────────┘

4.4 providers.tf - Provider Configuration

CORE FILE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

providers.tf configures how Terraform connects to external APIs. This includes cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), SaaS tools (GitHub, Datadog), and infrastructure platforms (Kubernetes, Docker). Separating provider configuration makes your code more maintainable, especially for multi-region or multi-account setups.

4.4.1 Provider Blocks

HCL providers.tf - Basic Configuration 
# ============================================================================
# providers.tf - Provider Configuration
# ============================================================================

# AWS Provider - Primary Region
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  # Best practice: Use environment variables or IAM roles for credentials
  # DO NOT hardcode credentials here!

  # Default tags applied to all resources
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = var.project_name
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    }
  }
}

# Azure Provider (if using Azure)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}

  subscription_id = var.azure_subscription_id
  tenant_id       = var.azure_tenant_id
}

# Google Cloud Provider (if using GCP)
provider "google" {
  project = var.gcp_project
  region  = var.gcp_region
}

# Kubernetes Provider (for K8s resources)
provider "kubernetes" {
  host                   = data.aws_eks_cluster.main.endpoint
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(data.aws_eks_cluster.main.certificate_authority[0].data)
  token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
}

4.4.2 Provider Aliases

Provider aliases allow multiple instances of the same provider with different configurations:

HCL Multi-Region with Aliases 
# Default AWS provider (us-east-1)
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  alias  = "primary"

  default_tags {
    tags = local.common_tags
  }
}

# Secondary region for DR
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-west-2"
  alias  = "secondary"

  default_tags {
    tags = local.common_tags
  }
}

# Europe region for data residency
provider "aws" {
  region = "eu-west-1"
  alias  = "europe"

  default_tags {
    tags = local.common_tags
  }
}

# Usage in resources:
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "primary_bucket" {
  provider = aws.primary
  bucket   = "${var.project_name}-primary"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "dr_bucket" {
  provider = aws.secondary
  bucket   = "${var.project_name}-dr"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "eu_bucket" {
  provider = aws.europe
  bucket   = "${var.project_name}-eu"
}
📝

EXAM TIP: Provider Alias Syntax

The alias creates a named provider: aws.secondary. Reference it in resources with provider = aws.secondary. Without an alias, resources use the default (un-aliased) provider.

4.4.3 Provider Feature Flags

Many providers have optional features you can enable:

HCL AWS Provider Features 
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  # Skip various account validity checks (useful for LocalStack)
  skip_credentials_validation = false
  skip_metadata_api_check     = false
  skip_region_validation      = false

  # Custom retry configuration
  retry_mode  = "standard"
  max_retries = 25

  # For testing with LocalStack
  # endpoints {
  #   s3  = "http://localhost:4566"
  #   ec2 = "http://localhost:4566"
  # }

  # Assume role for cross-account access
  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_ID:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "TerraformSession"
    external_id  = var.external_id
  }
}

4.5 terraform.tf - Terraform Settings Block

CORE FILE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

terraform.tf contains the terraform {} settings block—the meta-configuration that tells Terraform what version to use, which providers are required, where to store state, and other global settings. This ensures everyone on your team uses compatible versions.

4.5.1 Required Version

HCL terraform.tf - Version Constraints 
# ============================================================================
# terraform.tf - Terraform Configuration Settings
# ============================================================================

terraform {
  # Require specific Terraform version
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0, < 2.0.0"

  # Lock to specific minor version for stability
  # required_version = "~> 1.6.0"  # Allows 1.6.x but not 1.7.0
}
Constraint Meaning Example Versions Allowed
= 1.6.0 Exact version only 1.6.0 only
>= 1.5.0 Minimum version 1.5.0, 1.6.0, 2.0.0, etc.
~> 1.6.0 Pessimistic (patch only) 1.6.0, 1.6.1, 1.6.99 (not 1.7.0)
~> 1.6 Pessimistic (minor OK) 1.6.0, 1.7.0, 1.99.0 (not 2.0.0)
>= 1.5, < 2.0 Range 1.5.0 through 1.99.99

4.5.2 Required Providers

HCL Required Providers Block 
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    # AWS Provider
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }

    # Random Provider (for generating random values)
    random = {
      source  = "hashicorp/random"
      version = "~> 3.5"
    }

    # Null Provider (for provisioners and triggers)
    null = {
      source  = "hashicorp/null"
      version = "~> 3.2"
    }

    # Archive Provider (for creating zip files)
    archive = {
      source  = "hashicorp/archive"
      version = "~> 2.4"
    }

    # Kubernetes Provider
    kubernetes = {
      source  = "hashicorp/kubernetes"
      version = "~> 2.23"
    }

    # Helm Provider
    helm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/helm"
      version = "~> 2.11"
    }
  }
}
ℹ️

Provider Source Format

Format: namespace/name. HashiCorp providers use hashicorp/. Community providers use their maintainer's namespace, e.g., integrations/github.

4.5.3 Backend Configuration

The backend block defines where Terraform stores state:

HCL Backend Configuration 
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

  # S3 Backend (recommended for AWS)
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    key            = "project-name/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"  # For state locking
  }
}

# Alternative: HCP Terraform (Terraform Cloud)
# terraform {
#   cloud {
#     organization = "my-org"
#     workspaces {
#       name = "my-workspace"
#     }
#   }
# }

4.5.4 Experiments

Enable experimental features (use with caution):

HCL Experimental Features 
terraform {
  # Enable experimental language features
  experiments = [module_variable_optional_attrs]

  # Note: Experiments may change or be removed in future versions
}

4.5.5 Provider Meta-Arguments

HCL Provider Configuration Aliases 
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
      # Define configuration aliases for modules
      configuration_aliases = [aws.us_east, aws.eu_west]
    }
  }
}

4.6 locals.tf - Local Values

CORE FILE BEST PRACTICE
🎯

What You're Doing Here

locals.tf defines computed values you use throughout your configuration. Think of locals as variables whose values are computed from expressions rather than provided by users. They're perfect for DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) patterns like standard tags, computed names, and conditional logic.

4.6.1 Local Value Definitions

HCL locals.tf - Complete Example 
# ============================================================================
# locals.tf - Local Values (Computed Variables)
# ============================================================================

locals {
  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # NAMING CONVENTIONS
  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Consistent naming across all resources
  name_prefix = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}"

  # Full resource names
  vpc_name     = "${local.name_prefix}-vpc"
  cluster_name = "${local.name_prefix}-eks"
  bucket_name  = "${local.name_prefix}-assets-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"

  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # COMMON TAGS
  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Applied to all resources via provider default_tags or merge()
  common_tags = {
    Project     = var.project_name
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
    Team        = var.team_name
    CostCenter  = var.cost_center
    CreatedAt   = timestamp()
  }

  # Merge common tags with environment-specific tags
  all_tags = merge(local.common_tags, var.additional_tags)

  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # COMPUTED NETWORKING
  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # AZ count from subnet list
  az_count = length(var.availability_zones)

  # Calculate private subnet CIDRs based on VPC CIDR
  private_subnet_cidrs = [
    for i in range(local.az_count) :
    cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 4, i + local.az_count)
  ]

  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # CONDITIONAL LOGIC
  # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  # Production gets more instances
  instance_count = var.environment == "prod" ? 3 : 1

  # Enable detailed monitoring only in production
  enable_monitoring = var.environment == "prod"

  # Instance type based on environment
  instance_type = var.environment == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro"
}

4.6.2 Complex Computed Values

HCL Advanced Local Values 
locals {
  # Transform a list of objects
  instance_configs = [
    for idx, name in var.instance_names : {
      name          = name
      index         = idx
      az            = var.availability_zones[idx % length(var.availability_zones)]
      is_primary    = idx == 0
      instance_type = idx == 0 ? "t3.medium" : "t3.micro"
    }
  ]

  # Create a map from a list for for_each
  security_rules = {
    for rule in var.ingress_rules :
    "${rule.from_port}-${rule.to_port}-${rule.protocol}" => rule
  }

  # Flatten nested structures
  subnet_route_associations = flatten([
    for rt_key, rt in aws_route_table.private : [
      for subnet_key, subnet in aws_subnet.private : {
        route_table_id = rt.id
        subnet_id      = subnet.id
        key            = "${rt_key}-${subnet_key}"
      }
    ]
  ])

  # Environment-specific configuration map
  env_config = {
    dev = {
      instance_type    = "t3.micro"
      instance_count   = 1
      enable_monitoring = false
      backup_retention = 1
    }
    staging = {
      instance_type    = "t3.small"
      instance_count   = 2
      enable_monitoring = true
      backup_retention = 7
    }
    prod = {
      instance_type    = "t3.large"
      instance_count   = 3
      enable_monitoring = true
      backup_retention = 30
    }
  }

  # Get current environment config
  current_env_config = local.env_config[var.environment]
}

4.6.3 Tag Standardization

One of the most common uses of locals is standardizing tags:

🏷️ Tag Strategy with Locals 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     TAGGING STRATEGY                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  variables.tf          locals.tf              resources          │
│  ─────────────         ─────────              ─────────          │
│                                                                  │
│  var.project_name ──┐                                           │
│  var.environment  ──┼──► local.common_tags ──┬──► aws_vpc       │
│  var.team_name    ──┘         │              ├──► aws_subnet    │
│                               │              ├──► aws_instance  │
│                               ▼              └──► aws_rds       │
│                        ┌─────────────┐                           │
│  var.extra_tags   ────►│   merge()   │──────► local.all_tags    │
│                        └─────────────┘                           │
│                                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Usage in resources:

  resource "aws_instance" "web" {
    # ... config ...
    tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
      Name = "${local.name_prefix}-web"
      Role = "WebServer"
    })
  }
🔄
DRY Principle

Define tags once, use everywhere

✏️
Easy Updates

Change one local, update all resources

📊
Cost Tracking

Consistent tags enable accurate billing reports

🔍
Resource Discovery

Find resources by project, environment, team

4.7 data.tf - Data Sources

OPTIONAL FILE ORGANIZATION
🎯

What You're Doing Here

data.tf contains data source definitions—queries to fetch information about existing infrastructure. Some teams keep data sources in main.tf, but separating them makes it clear what you're querying vs. what you're creating.

4.7.1 Data Source Organization

HCL data.tf - Organized Data Sources 
# ============================================================================
# data.tf - Data Source Definitions
# ============================================================================
# Query existing infrastructure and external data.
# These are READ-ONLY - they don't create or modify anything.

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# AWS ACCOUNT & REGION INFO
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}

data "aws_region" "current" {}

data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# AMI LOOKUPS
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux_2" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

# Latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux_2023" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["al2023-ami-*-x86_64"]
  }
}

# Latest Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]  # Canonical

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
  }
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# EXISTING VPC (if referencing existing infrastructure)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
data "aws_vpc" "existing" {
  count = var.create_vpc ? 0 : 1

  tags = {
    Name = var.existing_vpc_name
  }
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SECRETS FROM AWS SECRETS MANAGER
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
data "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_credentials" {
  secret_id = var.db_secret_name
}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SSM PARAMETERS
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
data "aws_ssm_parameter" "db_host" {
  name = "/${var.environment}/database/host"
}

4.7.2 External Data Queries

Data sources for external information:

HCL External Data Sources 
# Read a local file
data "local_file" "user_data_script" {
  filename = "${path.module}/scripts/user-data.sh"
}

# Fetch data from HTTP endpoint
data "http" "my_ip" {
  url = "https://ipv4.icanhazip.com"
}

# Get remote Terraform state (from another workspace)
data "terraform_remote_state" "networking" {
  backend = "s3"

  config = {
    bucket = "terraform-state-bucket"
    key    = "networking/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Run external script
data "external" "version" {
  program = ["bash", "${path.module}/scripts/get-version.sh"]

  query = {
    environment = var.environment
  }
}

# Template file rendering
data "template_file" "user_data" {
  template = file("${path.module}/templates/user-data.tpl")

  vars = {
    environment = var.environment
    app_name    = var.project_name
  }
}

4.8 versions.tf - Version Constraints

ALTERNATIVE 📝 EXAM TOPIC
🎯

What You're Doing Here

versions.tf is an alternative location for version constraints. Some teams prefer putting the terraform {} block in terraform.tf, while others use versions.tf. What matters is consistency within your organization.

4.8.1 Terraform Version Constraints

HCL versions.tf Example 
# ============================================================================
# versions.tf - Version Constraints
# ============================================================================
# Alternative to terraform.tf - some teams prefer this file name.
# Contains only version constraints for Terraform and providers.

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5.0, < 2.0.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.31"
    }
    random = {
      source  = "hashicorp/random"
      version = "~> 3.6"
    }
  }
}
💡

Naming Convention

Choose either terraform.tf OR versions.tf for your organization—not both. The terraform {} block can appear in any .tf file, but consistency matters.

4.8.2 Provider Version Constraints

Constraint Pattern Use Case Risk Level
version = "5.31.0" Exact version pinning 🟢 Lowest - but misses security patches
version = "~> 5.31" Patch updates only 🟢 Low - RECOMMENDED
version = "~> 5.0" Minor updates allowed 🟡 Medium - may get breaking changes
version = ">= 5.0" Any version 5.0+ 🔴 High - could jump to 6.0
No constraint Latest version 🔴 Highest - unpredictable

4.8.3 Module Version Constraints

HCL Module Version Pinning 
# Pin module versions in main.tf (not versions.tf)
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.4.0"  # Exact version for reproducibility

  # ... module inputs
}

module "eks" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws"
  version = "~> 19.21"  # Allow patch updates

  # ... module inputs
}

# Git-based modules with tags
module "custom" {
  source = "git::https://github.com/org/terraform-module.git?ref=v1.2.0"
}

4.9 backend.tf - Backend Configuration

OPTIONAL FILE PRODUCTION
🎯

What You're Doing Here

backend.tf separates backend configuration from other settings. This is useful when you have different backends per environment or need to use partial configuration (providing values at init time).

HCL backend.tf - S3 Backend 
# ============================================================================
# backend.tf - Remote State Backend Configuration
# ============================================================================
# Separating backend config allows different backends per environment.

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    # Static values that don't change
    bucket         = "company-terraform-state"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"

    # Key varies per project/environment
    # Can be provided at init time: terraform init -backend-config="key=dev/app/terraform.tfstate"
    key = "project-name/environment/terraform.tfstate"
  }
}
🔒 S3 Backend Architecture
